citations.section: Chapter XII: It Might Have Been
citations.quote: We are going to secure an acquittal…the prosecution will receive a great surprise — Anstey visits Thorndyke's chambers

normalized_terms: chapter xii: it might have been

nodes.label: Chapter XV: The Finger-Print Experts

nodes.label: Chapter XVI: Thorndyke Plays His Card

citations.section: Chapter XVI: Thorndyke Plays His Card
citations.quote: In your opinion, was the mark upon that paper made by the thumb of the prisoner? No. I am convinced that it was not made by the thumb of Reuben Hornby... it was not made by a human thumb at all.

nodes.description: Chapter XVI: Thorndyke Plays His Card, the chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which Thorndyke presents his expert evidence in court and states definitively that the thumb-print on the stolen diamond safe was not made by the prisoner Reuben Hornby.

nodes.description: Chapter XVII: At Last is the concluding chapter of The Red Thumb Mark in which the jury returns a verdict of not guilty without retiring, the judge noting that Thorndyke has probably averted a serious miscarriage of justice.

nodes.label: Brigham Young

citations.quote: 'You shall remain here,' he said. 'In a few days you will have recovered from your fatigues. Brigham Young has said it, and he has spoken with the voice of Joseph Smith, which is the voice of God.'
Young speedily proved himself to be a skilful administrator as well as a resolute chief. Maps were drawn and charts prepared, in which the future city was sketched out.

One fine morning, John Ferrier was about to set out to his wheatfields, when he heard the click of the latch, and, looking through the window, saw a stout, sandy-haired, middle-aged man coming up the pathway. His heart leapt to his mouth, for this was none other than the great Brigham Young himself.

'It were better for you, John Ferrier,' he thundered, 'that you and she were now lying blanched skeletons upon the Sierra Blanco, than that you should put your weak wills against the orders of the Holy Four!'

nodes.description: Brigham Young, the Mormon leader in A Study in Scarlet who rescued the wandering Ferrier party and later demands John Ferrier give his daughter Lucy to one of the elders' sons, setting the tragic events in motion.

I had no difficulty in finding her rooms, which were central and well appointed. A maid showed me in without ceremony, and as I entered the sitting-room a lady, who was sitting before a Remington typewriter, sprang up with a pleasant smile of welcome.
Mrs. Laura Lyons wrote the letter and the appointment was made with the knowledge and consent of Stapleton.
He had promised to marry her in case she could get a divorce, and so she wrote the letter to lure Sir Charles to his death.

citations.quote: 'She's mine 'cause I saved her. No man will take her from me. She's Lucy Ferrier from this day on.'

Lucy Ferrier grew up within the log-house, and assisted her adopted father in all his undertakings. The keen air of the mountains and the balsamic odour of the pine trees took the place of nurse and mother.
'She has grown to be the flower of Utah, and has found favour in the eyes of many who are high in the land.'
The greeting between the lovers was warm, but brief, for minutes were precious, and there was much to be done.

poor Lucy never held up her head again, but pined away and died within a month. Her sottish husband, who had married her principally for the sake of John Ferrier's property, did not affect any great grief at his bereavement.
'What do you think of Lucy Ferrier now?' I cried, locking the door, and shaking the key in his face. 'Punishment has been slow in coming, but it has overtaken you at last.'

nodes.label: Mad Joe

citations.quote: the early worshipper was none other than the village idiot, a nephew of the blacksmith, one who neither would nor could care for the church or for anything else. He was always called 'Mad Joe'

nodes.description: Mad Joe, the village idiot who was the nephew of the blacksmith and the only early-morning worshipper at the church in the Father Brown story 'The Hammer of God'.

nodes.label: Madame Andermatt

citations.section: Story VI: The Seven of Hearts
citations.quote: Madame Andermatt had written the letter concealed in the cigar.

nodes.description: Madame Andermatt is the woman who authored the letter concealed inside a cigar, used to pass intelligence in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.

nodes.label: Madame Charpentier

citations.quote: Madame Charpentier was evidently seized with a fit of terror at the very idea.

nodes.description: Madame Charpentier is the proprietress of the Torquay Terrace boarding house in A Study in Scarlet where Enoch Drebber lodged; she is seized with terror when Gregson questions her about Drebber's movements.

nodes.label: Madame Grunov

citations.section: The Worst Crime in the World
citations.quote: That horrible fat woman with yellow hair is called Madame Grunov... a very dominant-looking lady, sensationally clad in scarlet, with a mane of yellow hair too long to be called bobbed.

nodes.description: Madame Grunov, a dominant, sensationally dressed woman with yellow hair who is the wife of Mr. Grunov in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.

nodes.label: Madame Renaud

citations.quote: Madame Renaud, wife of the prison inspector, who assisted Lupin in the train compartment.

nodes.description: Madame Renaud is the wife of the prison inspector who assists Arsène Lupin in a train compartment, enabling his escape in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.

nodes.label: Maggie MacNab

citations.quote: Why, what has happened, Maggie? James has been murdered, for all I can make out.

nodes.description: Maggie MacNab, a young woman who alarms Father Brown with the claim that James has been murdered in the Father Brown story 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.

nodes.label: Magnus

citations.quote: A man clad completely in black, even (it was remembered) to the dreadful detail of black gloves, appeared on the ridge above the engine

nodes.description: Magnus, a sinister figure clad entirely in black including black gloves who appears dramatically on a ridge in the Father Brown story 'The Three Tools of Death'.

nodes.label: Mahomet Singh

citations.quote: Jonathan Small, Mahomet Singh, Abdullah Khan, Dost Akbar
Mahomet Singh was left to guard the door

nodes.description: Mahomet Singh, one of the Four who signed the Agra treasure pact and was left to guard the door during the murder of Achmet in 'The Sign of the Four'.

nodes.label: Maître Detinan

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter I: Number 514, Series 23
citations.quote: The reporters rushed to interview Maitre Detinan, an influential radical lawyer who was known to be Arsene Lupin's legal adviser.

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter II: The Blue Diamond
citations.quote: Maitre Detinan maintained lawyer-client privilege even under pressure from Ganimard.

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter III: Holmlock Shears Opens Hostilities
citations.quote: Holmlock Shears called on Maitre Detinan at the Rue Clapeyron.

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter IV: A Glimmer in the Darkness
citations.quote: Detinan passed information to Shears about the blonde lady.

nodes.description: Maître Detinan is an influential radical lawyer who serves as Arsène Lupin's legal counsel, is interviewed by reporters, and firmly maintains lawyer-client privilege when pressured by both Ganimard and Holmlock Shears in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: Major Murray

citations.section: The Sign of the Broken Sword
citations.quote: a lean, dark-haired man, apparently, of the name of Murray--a north of Ireland man and a Puritan

nodes.description: Major Murray, a lean, dark-haired Ulster Protestant who served under General St. Clare and appears in the backstory of the Father Brown story 'The Sign of the Broken Sword'.

nodes.label: Major Putnam (Anglo-Indian)

citations.quote: the house which belonged to an Anglo-Indian Major named Putnam; and that the Major had a native cook from Malta who was of his communion.

nodes.description: Major Putnam, an Anglo-Indian who owns a house with a Maltese cook named Marco, the setting for the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.

nodes.label: Major Sholto

citations.quote: Major John Sholto, once of the Indian army, retired to Pondicherry Lodge

nodes.description: Major John Sholto, a former Indian Army officer who retired to Pondicherry Lodge, kept the secret of the Agra Treasure hidden from his sons until his deathbed, having betrayed Captain Morstan.

nodes.label: Mr. Mallow

citations.quote: A young man of the name of Mallow, very big and shy, with brown eyes like a dog's, dropped a cup and apologized awkwardly.

nodes.description: Mr. Mallow, a shy, big, brown-eyed young man who drops a cup and apologises awkwardly at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.

nodes.label: Malvoli (Italian fighter)

citations.quote: the great fight between Malvoli and Nigger Ned is coming off after all

nodes.description: Malvoli, an Italian prizefighter who is one of the two combatants in the championship bout at the centre of the Father Brown story 'The God of the Gongs'.

nodes.label: man with crooked/false nose

citations.quote: He is so extremely absent. I suppose there was never anybody so absent as Mr Glass.

nodes.description: The man with a crooked or false nose, a mysterious absent figure known only as 'Mr Glass', alluded to in the Father Brown story 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.

nodes.label: Marco

citations.quote: 'Marco, open the case.' The man beside him with the earrings and the big black case proceeded to unlock it.

nodes.description: Marco, a manservant with earrings who opens a large black case at Prince Saradine's command in the Father Brown story 'The Sins of Prince Saradine'.

nodes.label: Marco (Maltese cook)

citations.quote: Marco has all the sauces, and we've often done ourselves well in very rough places

nodes.description: Marco, the Maltese cook employed by Major Putnam, who is skilled with sauces and served the household in difficult circumstances in the Father Brown story 'The Salad of Colonel Cray'.

nodes.label: Maria Gibson (née Pinto)

citations.quote: his wife, Maria, a Brazilian by birth

nodes.description: Maria Gibson, née Pinto, is the jealous Brazilian-born wife of Neil Gibson who stages her own death on Thor Bridge to frame the governess Grace Dunbar for murder in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

nodes.label: Marie Devine

citations.section: The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax
citations.quote: Marie Devine, Lady Frances's maid

nodes.description: Marie Devine, Lady Frances Carfax's maid, whose sudden marriage provides a clue to the whereabouts of her mistress in 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax'.

nodes.label: Marlow Bates

citations.quote: Marlow Bates, the estate manager

nodes.description: Marlow Bates, the estate manager at Thor Place whose testimony assists Holmes in 'The Problem of Thor Bridge'.

nodes.label: Marquess of Montalva

citations.quote: the Marquess of Montalva, pursued by Murillo's enemies

nodes.description: The Marquess of Montalva, an alias assumed by Don Juan Murillo while being pursued by his political enemies in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge'.

nodes.label: Marquis of Marne (James Mair / Maurice Mair)

citations.quote: He was looking at the ground like a monk, and hidden in a black hood that was really as ridiculous as any mask... it isn't Jim at all ... it's Maurice!

nodes.description: The Marquis of Marne, a reclusive figure hidden in a black hood who is revealed to be Maurice Mair impersonating his dead cousin James in the Father Brown story 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
normalized_terms: marquis of marne (james mair / maurice mair)

nodes.label: Martha (Holmes's housekeeper)

citations.section: His Last Bow: The War Service of Sherlock Holmes
citations.quote: Martha, the old housekeeper who had served Von Bork for five years

nodes.description: Martha is the elderly housekeeper Holmes plants inside Von Bork's household in His Last Bow, serving the German spy for five years while feeding intelligence to Holmes.

nodes.label: Mary Maberley

citations.quote: Mrs. Mary Maberley, widow of the late Douglas Maberley

nodes.description: Mrs. Mary Maberley is the widow of Douglas Maberley in The Adventure of the Three Gables whose house is burgled by Isadora Klein's agents seeking to steal the manuscript novel Douglas wrote about his affair with Klein before his death.

nodes.label: Mary Morstan

citations.quote: Miss Mary Morstan entered the room with a firm step
The treasure is lost, said Miss Morstan, calmly

nodes.description: Mary Morstan, the calm and resourceful young governess who consults Holmes in The Sign of the Four about mysterious annual pearls she has received, and whose loss of the Agra Treasure she accepts calmly so she can marry Watson.

nodes.label: Miss Mary Sutherland

citations.quote: "Miss Mary Sutherland, of Lower Brixton Road," said Holmes, as he looked from my client to myself.

nodes.description: Miss Mary Sutherland of Lower Brixton Road, a guileless young woman deceived by her own stepfather disguised as her fiance Hosmer Angel in the Sherlock Holmes story 'A Case of Identity'.

nodes.label: Mason the Plate-layer

citations.section: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans
citations.quote: Mason the plate-layer who found the body

nodes.description: Mason the plate-layer, a railway worker who discovered the body of Arthur Cadogan West on the Underground tracks in 'The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans'.

nodes.label: The Master of the Mountain

citations.quote: the Master of the Mountain, that world-famous fortune-teller by crystals and chiromancy... His pointed and finely finished features seemed moulded by some skilful craftsman as a mask of copper.

nodes.description: The Master of the Mountain, a world-famous fortune-teller by crystal and chiromancy with a refined, pointed face, who appears at the gathering in the Father Brown story 'The Red Moon of Meru'.

nodes.label: Master Vatinel

citations.quote: 'Oh, I knew it at once. It belonged to Master Vatinel, the carter,' said the witness.

nodes.description: Master Vatinel is the carter identified by a witness as the owner of the vehicle involved in key events in The Hollow Needle (Arsène Lupin).

nodes.label: Maud Bellamy

citations.quote: Maud Bellamy, who was secretly engaged to McPherson

nodes.description: Maud Bellamy, the young woman secretly engaged to the drowned Fitzroy McPherson, whose grief and courage impress Holmes in 'The Adventure of the Lion's Mane'.

nodes.label: Maurice Blum

citations.quote: Maurice Blum started out as an anarchist of principle, a father of the poor; he ended a greasy spy and tale-bearer

nodes.description: Maurice Blum, a man who began as a sincere anarchist and champion of the poor but degenerated into a spy and informer, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Flying Stars'.

nodes.label: M. Maurice Brun

citations.quote: M. Maurice Brun and M. Armand Armagnac were crossing the sunlit Champs Elysee

nodes.description: M. Maurice Brun, a French intellectual and companion of M. Armagnac, who crosses the Champs-Elysees with him at the opening of the Father Brown story 'The Duel of Dr Hirsch'.

nodes.label: Maurice Mair (the false Marquis, the impersonator)

citations.quote: Maurice Mair was certainly a wonder. James was no fool, and very good at his own political job; but it seems that Maurice could do that and everything else; that he was a brilliant artist and amateur actor and musician.

nodes.description: Maurice Mair, the brilliant impersonator who took the place of his dead cousin James Mair as the Marquis of Marne and lived in seclusion, revealed by Father Brown in 'The Chief Mourner of Marne'.
normalized_terms: maurice mair (the false marquis, the impersonator)

nodes.label: Maxime Bermond

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter IV: A Glimmer in the Darkness
citations.quote: 'Maxime Bermond!' cried M. Destange, in delight. 'My dear Maxime!...'

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter V: Kidnapped
citations.quote: Maxime Bermond was Lupin's alias in the Destange household.

nodes.description: Maxime Bermond, the alias under which Arsene Lupin endeared himself to Clotilde Destange and her household in the story 'The Blonde Lady'.

nodes.label: McMurdo

citations.quote: Not Mr. Sherlock Holmes! How could I have mistook you?
McMurdo had been arrested as an accessory

nodes.description: McMurdo is the ex-prizefighter gatekeeper at Pondicherry Lodge in The Sign of the Four who recognises Holmes from a bout, allows entry, and is later arrested as an accessory to Bartholomew Sholto's murder.

nodes.label: Young Medlicott

citations.quote: young Medlicott was involved

nodes.description: Young Medlicott, a peripheral figure involved in the events of the Raffles story 'A Bad Night' from 'A Thief in the Night'.

nodes.label: Menzies (Mine Engineer)

citations.quote: the mine engineer, a great bearded Scotchman named Menzies

nodes.description: Menzies is the great bearded Scottish mine engineer at Crow Hill in Vermissa Valley who witnesses the Scowrers' violence in Part II, Chapter V of The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Detective Merton

citations.quote: the young detective (whose name was Merton) led the little priest across the fields to the railway

nodes.description: Detective Merton, a young detective who leads Father Brown across the fields to the railway at the conclusion of 'The Three Tools of Death'.

nodes.label: Michael Flood

citations.quote: 'Michael Flood,' said the stranger in a snappy fashion. He was an unnaturally lean little man, with a hooked nose too large for his face, which was colourless, like parchment, in contrast with the ginger colour of his hair.

nodes.description: Michael Flood, an unnaturally lean man with a hooked nose who identifies himself in a snappy manner during the investigation in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.

nodes.label: Michael Moonshine

citations.quote: a once famous criminal, known as Michael Moonshine, and many other names that were presumably not his own, had recently been released after a long term of imprisonment for his numerous burglaries.

nodes.description: Michael Moonshine, a once-famous criminal operating under many aliases who has recently been released and is suspected of planning a robbery in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.

nodes.label: Michel Beaumont

citations.section: Chapter II: Eight from Nine Leaves One
citations.quote: Michel Beaumont — one of Lupin's aliases used during the crystal stopper investigation.

citations.section: Chapter IV: The Chief of the Enemies
citations.quote: Michel Beaumont presented himself to Clarisse as a journalist.

nodes.description: Michel Beaumont, an alias used by Arsene Lupin during the crystal stopper investigation, under which he presented himself to Clarisse as a journalist.

nodes.label: Mike Scanlan

citations.quote: I'm Brother Scanlan, Lodge 341, Vermissa Valley
Mike Scanlan
Mike Scanlan

nodes.description: Mike Scanlan, a member of Lodge 341 of the Scowrers in Vermissa Valley in The Valley of Fear, who befriends the newly arrived McMurdo and introduces him to the criminal fraternity's Molly Maguires-like organisation.

nodes.label: Miles McLaren

citations.section: The Adventure of the Three Students
citations.quote: Miles McLaren

nodes.description: Miles McLaren, one of the three scholarship candidates at St. Luke's College, named in connection with the examination paper theft in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Three Students'.

nodes.label: Milverton's Avenger (unnamed noblewoman)

citations.section: The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton
citations.quote: It is I, the woman whose life you have ruined

nodes.description: Milverton's avenger is the unnamed noblewoman who shoots Charles Augustus Milverton dead at his desk in The Adventure of Charles Augustus Milverton, avenging his blackmail that drove her first husband to his death.

nodes.label: Minna Adler

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: Minna Adler — victim; German girl from Bremen; waitress at Empire Restaurant, Fenchurch Street; throat cut while sleeping

nodes.description: Minna Adler is a German girl from Bremen working as a waitress at the Empire Restaurant on Fenchurch Street who is found murdered with her throat cut while sleeping, serving as the victim in a case from John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Miriam Goldstein

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: Miriam Goldstein — daughter; red-haired; short, powerful, wild-eyed; accused and arrested; stencil-cutter for decorators; passionate and jealous; INNOCENT

nodes.description: Miriam Goldstein is a red-haired, short, powerfully built, wild-eyed stencil-cutter for decorators who is accused and arrested in connection with the same case as murder victim Minna Adler in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Miss Barlow (typist)

citations.quote: Miss Barlow, take down a letter to Mr Finn, please.

nodes.description: Miss Barlow, the editor's typist who is instructed to take down a letter to Francis Finn in the Father Brown story 'The Purple Wig'.

nodes.label: Miss Burnet (governess)

citations.quote: Miss Burnet, the governess at High Gable

nodes.description: Miss Burnet is the governess at High Gable who assists in exposing Don Juan Murillo, the Tiger of San Pedro, and is connected to Victor Durando's revolutionary network in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

nodes.label: Miss Dobney

citations.section: The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax
citations.quote: Miss Dobney, the companion, who brought the case to our notice

nodes.description: Miss Dobney, the companion of Lady Frances Carfax, who first brought the case of her employer's disappearance to Holmes's attention in 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax'.

nodes.label: Mme. Henri Fournaye (Lucas's wife)

citations.section: The Adventure of the Second Stain
citations.quote: Mme. Henri Fournaye, occupying a small villa in the Rue Austerlitz

nodes.description: Mme. Henri Fournaye is the Creole wife who occupies a villa in the Rue Austerlitz in Paris and murders her husband Eduardo Lucas in his London lodgings on discovering his double life, complicating Holmes's Second Stain investigation.

nodes.label: Mordecai Smith

citations.quote: Mordecai Smith was printed across it in large letters
I want her to-night at eight o'clock sharp

nodes.description: Mordecai Smith, the owner of the steam launch Aurora, whose boat was hired by Jonathan Small for the Thames chase in 'The Sign of the Four'.

nodes.label: Mortimer Tregennis

citations.section: The Adventure of the Devil's Foot
citations.quote: Mortimer Tregennis came to see us in Holmes's little villa

nodes.description: Mortimer Tregennis is the man who comes to Holmes's Sussex villa to report that his brothers and sister were found mad or dead after an evening at home, and who is himself later poisoned by Dr. Leon Sterndale in The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.

nodes.label: Moser

citations.quote: Moser

nodes.description: Moser, a figure briefly mentioned in the general background of the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax'.

nodes.label: Mr. Atkinson

citations.quote: 'It's no good, Mr. Atkinson,' said the doctor… the young man in the billycock

nodes.description: Mr. Atkinson, a young man in a billycock hat who is told by the doctor to leave the dying man's room in the Father Brown story 'The Wrong Shape'.

nodes.label: Mr. Audley

citations.quote: Its president was Mr. Audley… an amiable, elderly man who still wore Gladstone collars

nodes.description: Mr. Audley, the amiable, elderly president of the Twelve True Fishermen's club who still wears Gladstone collars in the Father Brown story 'The Queer Feet'.

nodes.label: Mr. Brodribb

citations.quote: Mr. Brodribb — portly elderly solicitor; old-school manner; Weybridge neighbour of Alfred Calverley; Thorndyke's old friend

nodes.description: Mr. Brodribb is a portly, elderly, old-school solicitor who is both a Weybridge neighbour of Alfred Calverley and a long-standing friend of Thorndyke, through whom he introduces clients to the doctor in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Mr Glass

citations.quote: I know his name is Glass. I heard it through the door.

nodes.description: Mr Glass, a mysterious and apparently absent individual whose name is overheard through a door and whose existence puzzles Dr. Hood in the Father Brown story 'The Absence of Mr Glass'.

nodes.label: Mr. Grunov (Madame Grunov's husband)

citations.section: The Worst Crime in the World
citations.quote: that little man passes as her husband... a short man with a big beard and a very broad face, with long sleepy slits of eyes.

nodes.description: Mr. Grunov, a short man with a big beard and broad, sleepy-eyed face who passes as Madame Grunov's husband in the Father Brown story 'The Worst Crime in the World'.

nodes.label: Mr. Hurst

citations.quote: Mr. Hurst — John Bellingham's cousin; florid, good-looking man; a businessman; Eltham house

nodes.description: Mr. Hurst is John Bellingham's cousin, described as a florid, good-looking businessman who owns a house in Eltham and appears among the parties involved in the disappearance case at the centre of The Vanishing Man (Dr Thorndyke).

nodes.label: Mr. Jellicoe

citations.quote: Mr. Jellicoe — solicitor; John Bellingham's lawyer and agent — a small, spare man, with a face like a mummy, a dry, precise manner and gold-rimmed spectacles

nodes.description: Mr. Jellicoe, the solicitor and agent for John Bellingham, a small, spare, mummy-faced man of dry and precise manner who appears in Dr. Thorndyke's 'The Vanishing Man'.

nodes.label: Mr Justice Monkhouse

citations.quote: The trial was presided over by Mr Justice Monkhouse, one of those who are jeered at as humorous judges

nodes.description: Mr Justice Monkhouse, a judge with a reputation for humorous remarks who presides over the trial in the Father Brown story 'The Man in the Passage'.

nodes.label: Mr. Lawley

citations.quote: Come in, Mr. Lawley, said Thorndyke, rather stiffly, and, as he held the door open, the two visitors entered.

nodes.description: Mr. Lawley is a visitor received rather stiffly by Thorndyke in The Red Thumb Mark, one of the Dr Thorndyke stories.

nodes.label: Mr. Lever

citations.quote: The existing owner of the hotel was a Jew named Lever; and he made nearly a million out of it

nodes.description: Mr. Lever, the Jewish owner of the hotel who made nearly a million pounds from it, mentioned in the Father Brown story 'The Queer Feet'.

nodes.label: Mr. Marchmont

citations.quote: I have brought a client of mine to see you, and when I tell you that his name is Solomon Löwe, it will be unnecessary for me to say what our business is.
Mr. Marchmont — Thorndyke's solicitor friend; waited at Hartridge's flat; told Thorndyke about Wolfe; brought handwriting specimen

nodes.description: Mr. Marchmont is a solicitor and friend of Thorndyke who waited at Hartridge's flat, informed Thorndyke about Wolfe, and brought handwriting evidence, and who also introduces the distressed client Solomon Löwe to Thorndyke in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Mr. Maturin

citations.quote: Mr. Maturin was the occupant

nodes.description: Mr. Maturin, the occupant of the rooms targeted in the Raffles story 'No Sinecure' from 'Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman'.

nodes.label: Mr. Melas (Greek interpreter)

citations.quote: Melas... I am a Greek by birth and interpreter by profession

nodes.description: Mr. Melas is a professional Greek interpreter who is forcibly abducted and compelled to translate for a captive Greek man, serving as the client at the centre of The Adventure of the Greek Interpreter in the Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: Mr Patrick Butler K.C.

citations.quote: The prisoner was defended by Mr Patrick Butler, K.C., who was mistaken for a mere flaneur

nodes.description: Mr Patrick Butler K.C., the barrister who defended the prisoner and was deceptively underestimated as a mere socialite in the Father Brown story 'The Man in the Passage'.

nodes.label: Mr. Roundhay (vicar)

citations.section: The Adventure of the Devil's Foot
citations.quote: Mr. Roundhay, the vicar at Poldhu Bay

nodes.description: Mr. Roundhay, the vicar at Poldhu Bay who alerts Holmes to the death of Brenda Tregennis in the Sherlock Holmes story 'The Adventure of the Devil's Foot'.

nodes.label: Henry James Singleton

citations.section: Chapter III: A Lady in the Case
citations.quote: a sedate-looking gentleman seated at a large writing table. How-d'ye-do, doctor? said the latter, rising and holding out his hand. I can guess what you've come for. Want to see that thumb-print, eh?
It is the print of the left thumb of Reuben Hornby, the prisoner at the bar.

nodes.description: Henry James Singleton is the Scotland Yard fingerprint expert who testifies at trial that the incriminating thumbprint belongs to the accused Reuben Hornby, only to be proven wrong by Thorndyke's analysis in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: Mr. Warren

citations.section: The Adventure of the Red Circle
citations.quote: Mr. Warren, the landlord of 221 Great Orme Street

nodes.description: Mr. Warren, the landlord of 221 Great Orme Street whose mysterious lodger draws Holmes into the case in 'The Adventure of the Red Circle'.

nodes.label: Mr. Williams (mentioned)

citations.quote: something that De Quincey says about Mr. Williams, that ghastly criminal who slaughtered two whole families almost in silence.

nodes.description: Mr. Williams, the notorious murderer cited by De Quincey as having slaughtered two entire families almost in silence, recalled by a character in the Father Brown story 'The Mirror of the Magistrate'.

nodes.label: Mrs. Allen (Housekeeper)

citations.quote: Mrs. Allen, a buxom and cheerful person
Mrs. Allen

nodes.description: Mrs. Allen is the buxom and cheerful housekeeper at Birlstone Manor House in The Valley of Fear, questioned by Holmes and Watson during their investigation in Part I, Chapters III and V.

nodes.label: Mrs. Amberley (née unknown)

citations.quote: a woman twenty years younger than himself — a good-looking woman

nodes.description: Mrs. Amberley is the good-looking woman twenty years younger than Josiah Amberley who eloped with Dr. Ray Ernest and was murdered with him by her husband in a gas chamber in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

nodes.label: Mrs. Anthony

citations.quote: This latter lady went by the name of Mrs. Anthony, but she spoke with a slight Italian accent

nodes.description: Mrs. Anthony, a woman with a slight Italian accent who lives at Prince Saradine's island retreat in the Father Brown story 'The Sins of Prince Saradine'.

nodes.label: Mrs. Bankes (Simon's wife)

citations.quote: Then there was his wife, a thin, dark lady. There was a sort of acid elegance about her.

nodes.description: Mrs. Bankes, a thin, dark woman of acid elegance married to Simon Bankes, appearing in the Father Brown story 'The Man with Two Beards'.

nodes.label: Mrs. Barnes (blacksmith's wife)

citations.quote: a magnificent woman with red-gold hair, was sobbing blindly on a bench

nodes.description: Mrs. Barnes, the magnificent red-gold-haired wife of the blacksmith, found sobbing on a bench at the scene of the crime in the Father Brown story 'The Hammer of God'.

nodes.label: Mrs. Eliza Barrymore

citations.quote: To him it may have been an unmitigated relief, but she wept bitterly in her apron.

after breakfast I met Mrs. Barrymore in the long corridor with the sun full upon her face. She was a large, impassive, heavy-featured woman with a stern set expression of mouth. But her telltale eyes were red and glanced at me from between swollen lids.
"There are only two women in the house, Sir Henry," he answered. "One is the scullery-maid...The other is my wife"

nodes.description: Mrs. Eliza Barrymore, the housekeeper of Baskerville Hall whose secret nightly signalling to her escaped convict brother Selden, a fugitive on the moor, is discovered by Watson.

nodes.label: Mrs. Bernstone

citations.quote: I have seen Mr. Bartholomew Sholto in joy and sorrow for ten long years

nodes.description: Mrs. Bernstone is the housekeeper at Pondicherry Lodge who had served Bartholomew Sholto in joy and sorrow for ten years and finds his body at the opening of The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Mrs Boulnois (née actress)

citations.section: The Strange Crime of John Boulnois
citations.quote: Boulnois had married a beautiful and not unsuccessful actress, to whom he was devoted in his own shy and ponderous style

nodes.description: Mrs Boulnois, a beautiful and formerly successful actress to whom the reserved scholar John Boulnois is devoted, whose presence attracts scandal in 'The Strange Crime of John Boulnois'.

nodes.label: Mrs. Cecil Forrester

citations.quote: you once enabled my employer, Mrs. Cecil Forrester, to unravel a complication
Mrs. Forrester had sat up in the hope of her return

nodes.description: Mrs. Cecil Forrester, the employer for whom Mary Morstan worked as a governess and who waited up for her return in 'The Sign of the Four'.

nodes.label: Mrs. Ferguson (Peruvian Wife)

citations.quote: his Peruvian wife — a dark, passionate woman

nodes.description: Mrs. Ferguson, the Peruvian-born wife of Robert Ferguson, a dark and passionate woman whose seemingly vampiric behaviour Holmes investigates in 'The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire'.

nodes.label: Mrs. Arabella Hornby

citations.section: Chapter III: A Lady in the Case
citations.quote: it seems that some months ago Mrs. Hornby, the wife of John Hornby, purchased one of these toys—a Thumbograph
I am sure Dr. Thorndyke will be very much interested to see this little book of yours, said I, with a view to stemming the tide of her reflections.

nodes.description: Mrs. Arabella Hornby is the wife of John Hornby who purchased a Thumbograph toy several months before the central events of The Red Thumb Mark, a detail that proves significant to Thorndyke's investigation.

nodes.label: Mrs Hudson

citations.quote: Our landlady brought up a card.
Mrs. Hudson opened the door to usher in two robust individuals
Quite so. Well, Mrs. Turner has my orders that you are to be shown up at once when you call.

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: Mrs. Hudson has made some change in that figure eight times
Ask the young lady to step up, Mrs. Hudson
Mrs. Hudson

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dying Detective
citations.quote: Mrs. Hudson, the landlady of Sherlock Holmes, was a longsuffering woman

citations.section: VIII. The Adventure of the Speckled Band
citations.quote: Mrs. Hudson has been knocked up, she retorted upon me, and I on you

nodes.description: Mrs Hudson, Sherlock Holmes's long-suffering landlady at 221B Baker Street who announces callers, admits visitors, and assists Holmes in the Dying Detective ruse, referred to variously as Mrs Turner in early adventures.

citations.quote: Rose Spender, the dead woman in the coffin

nodes.description: Rose Spender, an elderly woman found drugged in a coffin, the intended murder victim in The Disappearance of Lady Frances Carfax (Sherlock Holmes).

nodes.label: Rosenthall

citations.quote: Rosenthall's the man, and his house is worth visiting

nodes.description: Rosenthall, a wealthy man whose house Raffles regards as a prize target in A Costume Piece (The Amateur Cracksman).

we came upon the body. The man lay upon his face, his arms out, his fingers dug into the ground, and his features convulsed with some strong emotion.
Selden is Barrymore's wife's brother, the Notting Hill murderer.

the relations between young Baskerville and his neighbours or any fresh particulars concerning the death of Sir Charles.

nodes.description: The arrest of Jefferson Hope, the dramatic capture of the American avenger of the murdered Lucy Ferrier on Enoch Drebber's cab in Baker Street, achieved with Holmes's assistance.

nodes.label: The Death of Jefferson Hope

citations.quote: The aneurism had burst during the night... He had died with a placid smile upon his face.

nodes.description: The death of Jefferson Hope in custody, who died peacefully overnight of a burst aortic aneurism the night before he could be tried, in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Jefferson Hope's Pursuit of Vengeance

citations.quote: I have followed them through two continents until I caught them. They thought that they would tire me out, but they could not do it.

nodes.description: Jefferson Hope's pursuit of vengeance, the years-long chase across two continents in which Hope tracked Drebber and Stangerson through Europe and America to avenge the deaths of John and Lucy Ferrier before cornering them in London.

nodes.label: The Legend of Hugo Baskerville

citations.quote: it is with much reluctance that I describe the particulars of a tradition which has so long been associated with the name of Sir Hugo Baskerville
Hugo stood staring, and suddenly a great black beast shaped like a hound sprang upon his body.

nodes.description: The legend of Hugo Baskerville, an ancestor whose cruelty unleashed a spectral hound that tore out his throat, founding the Baskerville curse in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Indian Mutiny 1857

attributes.significance: context for Small's backstory and the theft of the Agra treasure

citations.quote: suddenly, without a note of warning, the great mutiny broke upon us

nodes.description: The Indian Mutiny of 1857, the historical uprising during which Jonathan Small and his confederates committed the murder of Achmet and seized the Agra treasure in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: The Murder of John Ferrier

citations.quote: There, in the centre of it, lay the unfortunate farmer. His face was deadly white, and yet there was no sign of recent agony upon his features.

nodes.description: The Murder of John Ferrier is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which John Ferrier is found dead with a white face and no wound, killed by the Danite Band for refusing to give Lucy to Drebber or Stangerson.

nodes.label: Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee 1897

citations.quote: the Diamond Jubilee of her Majesty

nodes.description: Queen Victoria's Diamond Jubilee of 1897, during which Raffles exploits the festivities as cover for a nocturnal theft from the British Museum in Raffles: Further Adventures.

nodes.label: Killing of the Hound

citations.quote: Five shots from Holmes and five from me—I emptied my barrel—and the monster was dead.

nodes.description: The Killing of the Hound is the climax in The Hound of the Baskervilles in which Holmes and Watson fire ten shots between them to bring down Stapleton's phosphorescent hound as it attacks Sir Henry on the Dartmoor moor.

nodes.label: McMurdo's Lodge Initiation

citations.quote: McMurdo branded and sworn in

nodes.description: The Lodge initiation of John McMurdo (Birdy Edwards's alias) into the Vermissa Valley chapter of the Scowrers, a secret society modelled on the Molly Maguires, in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Lucy Ferrier's Forced Marriage to Drebber

citations.quote: A month after their return to Salt Lake City... Lucy Ferrier was led to the altar of the Endowment House by Enoch Drebber.

nodes.description: Lucy Ferrier's Forced Marriage to Drebber is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which Lucy is led to the altar of the Endowment House a month after returning to Salt Lake City, forced by Mormon law to marry Enoch Drebber against her will.
normalized_terms: lucy ferrier's forced marriage to drebber

nodes.label: The Death of Lucy Ferrier

citations.quote: Within a month she faded and died. Her brutal husband... had not attempted to disguise his satisfaction.

nodes.description: The Death of Lucy Ferrier is the event in A Study in Scarlet in which Lucy, forced into marriage with Enoch Drebber, fades and dies within a month of the marriage, her brutal husband not disguising his satisfaction at her end.

nodes.label: The Mormon Migration to Utah

citations.quote: Driven from Nauvoo on the Mississippi, they had built their city in this remote spot.

nodes.description: The Mormon Migration to Utah is the historical backstory in A Study in Scarlet in which the Latter-day Saints, driven from Nauvoo on the Mississippi, make their great westward journey and build their city in the desert.

nodes.label: Holmes and Watson Move to 221B Baker Street

citations.quote: That very evening we moved our things round from the hotel, and on the following morning Sherlock Holmes followed me with several boxes and portmanteaus.

nodes.description: The occasion on which Holmes and Watson move their joint belongings to 221B Baker Street after agreeing to share lodgings, establishing their famous address in A Study in Scarlet.
normalized_terms: holmes and watson move to 221b baker street

nodes.label: Death of Raffles at Spion Kop

citations.quote: Raffles fell at Spion Kop

nodes.description: The Death of Raffles at Spion Kop is the event in The Knees of the Gods from Raffles: Further Adventures of the Amateur Cracksman in which A. J. Raffles is killed in action during the Boer War battle at Spion Kop, South Africa.
normalized_terms: death of raffles at spion kop

nodes.label: Trial of the Scowrers

citations.quote: McGinty met his fate upon the scaffold

nodes.description: The Trial of the Scowrers, the legal proceeding in The Valley of Fear at which Boss McGinty and other leaders of the criminal fraternity in Vermissa Valley were convicted, with McGinty meeting his fate on the scaffold.

nodes.label: The Second Arrest of Arsène Lupin

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter VI: The Second Arrest of Arsene Lupin
citations.quote: Ganimard arrested Lupin for the second time under the alias Felix Davey.

nodes.description: The second arrest of Arsène Lupin, made by Ganimard under the alias Félix Davey, a key turning point in The Blonde Lady.
normalized_terms: the second arrest of arsène lupin

nodes.label: Beating of Editor James Stanger

citations.quote: James Stanger of the Herald beaten
aftermath of Stanger beating

nodes.description: The Beating of Editor James Stanger is the Scowrer act of intimidation in Part II, Chapter III of The Valley of Fear in which the editor of the Vermissa Herald is assaulted for his editorial opposition to the lodge.

nodes.label: Stapleton's Disappearance in the Mire

citations.quote: Where then? There is unhappy evidence that he made for the Grimpen Mire, and his fate is still a mystery. The footprints showed that he had run towards it, and then—where? The mire has swallowed him.

nodes.description: Stapleton's disappearance into the treacherous Grimpen Mire on the night the hound is destroyed, leaving no body and only fading footprints as evidence in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Trapping of Birdy Edwards

citations.quote: McGinty plans the trap
Yes. Birdy Edwards is here. I am Birdy Edwards!

nodes.description: The unmasking and attempted trapping of Birdy Edwards (Pinkerton's undercover detective), which he turns the tables on, exposing and destroying the Scowrers in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Watson Meets Holmes

citations.quote: Dr. Watson, Mr. Sherlock Holmes, said Stamford, introducing us.

nodes.description: The first meeting of Dr. Watson and Sherlock Holmes at St. Bartholomew's Hospital, arranged by Stamford, marking the beginning of their celebrated partnership at 221B Baker Street.

nodes.label: Boccaccio's Decameron

citations.quote: A pocket edition of Boccaccio's 'Decameron' lay in the pocket, with the name of Joseph Stangerson upon the fly-leaf.

nodes.description: A pocket edition of Boccaccio's Decameron bearing Stangerson's name on the fly-leaf, found in the victim's pocket and used to identify him in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Changed Footstep Pattern of Sir Charles

citations.quote: "He was running, Watson—running desperately, running for his life, running until he burst his heart and fell dead upon his face."

nodes.description: The changed footstep pattern of Sir Charles Baskerville — switching from a walk to a desperate run — which Holmes reads as proof of terror before death in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
normalized_terms: changed footstep pattern of sir charles

nodes.label: The Cleveland Telegram

citations.quote: I had a telegram from the head of the police organisation of Cleveland.

nodes.description: A telegram from the head of the Cleveland police organisation that confirms the identity and background of Enoch Drebber, a key piece of evidence in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Drebber's Visiting Cards

citations.quote: ENOCH J. DREBBER, CLEVELAND, OHIO, U.S.A., was printed in gilt letters upon his cards.

nodes.description: Enoch J. Drebber's visiting cards, printed in gilt letters with his Cleveland, Ohio address, used by Holmes to establish the victim's identity in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Giant Hound Footprints near Body

citations.quote: "The marks were some twenty yards from the body and no one gave them a thought." "And you saw them?" "As clearly as I see you." "And you said nothing?" "What was the use?"

nodes.description: Giant hound footprints found twenty yards from Sir Charles's body, noticed only by Holmes and pointing to the supernatural menace in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Strong Paint Smell Masking Gas

citations.quote: Why should this man at such a time be filling his house with strong odours? To cover some other smell

nodes.description: The strong smell of fresh paint in Josiah Amberley's house, identified by Holmes as a cover for the odour of gas used to murder two victims in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

nodes.label: The Pill Box (found in Stangerson's room)

citations.quote: A small chip ointment box containing a couple of pills.

nodes.description: A small ointment box containing two pills, found in Stangerson's room; Holmes proves one pill is harmless and the other deadly in A Study in Scarlet.
normalized_terms: the pill box (found in stangerson's room)

nodes.label: The Poison Pills (Evidence)

citations.quote: One of these you may take and the other I will. There shall be no poison in the affair beyond what God's hand directs.

nodes.description: Two identical pills offered by the murderer Jefferson Hope to his captive victim, one deadly and one harmless, leaving the outcome to chance in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: The Word RACHE Written in Blood

citations.quote: Just above it, traced in blood-red letters upon the wall, was a single word—RACHE.

nodes.description: The Word RACHE Written in Blood is the cryptic clue found on the wall at 3 Lauriston Gardens in A Study in Scarlet — traced in blood-red letters, it is Jefferson Hope's intentional misdirection spelling the German word for revenge.
normalized_terms: the word rache written in blood

nodes.label: Raffles' Burglary Tools (Evidence)

citations.quote: the tools of his trade displayed in the Black Museum

nodes.description: Raffles's burglar tools, preserved and displayed in the Black Museum at Scotland Yard as relics of the famous criminal in Raffles: A Thief in the Night.

nodes.label: Trichinopoly Cigar Ash

citations.quote: smoked a Trichinopoly cigar. The ash was scattered over the floor.

nodes.description: Trichinopoly cigar ash scattered across the floor at the murder scene, used by Holmes to characterise the murderer in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Unused Haymarket Theatre Ticket

citations.quote: neither B thirty nor thirty-two had been occupied that night

nodes.description: Two unused seats (B30 and B32) at the Haymarket Theatre on the night of the murders, proving Amberley's alibi was fabricated in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

nodes.label: Wall Message 'We we—' (Indelible Pencil)

citations.quote: scribbled with a purple indelible pencil: 'We we----' That's all

nodes.description: An unfinished message — 'We we—' — scrawled in purple indelible pencil on the gas-chamber wall by a dying victim, found by Holmes in The Adventure of the Retired Colourman.

nodes.label: The Gold Wedding Ring (Evidence)

citations.quote: FOUND in the Brixton Road this morning, a plain gold wedding ring, picked up in the roadway.

nodes.description: A plain gold wedding ring found in the Brixton Road and advertised in the newspapers, used by Holmes as a lure in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Cigarette Stub Identified by Scent

citations.quote: "if you seriously desire to deceive me you must change your tobacconist; for when I see the stub of a cigarette marked Bradley, Oxford Street, I know that my friend Watson is in the neighbourhood."

nodes.description: The distinctive white Jessamine scent on a cigarette stub, identified by Holmes as conclusive evidence of Stapleton's identity in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Beryl 'Stapleton' is Actually Stapleton's Wife

citations.quote: Mrs. Stapleton is none other than his wife.
His wife—for the woman was his wife—was a Costa Rican woman of the name of Beryl Garcia.

nodes.description: The revealed fact that 'Miss' Beryl Stapleton is actually Stapleton's wife, born Beryl Garcia in Costa Rica, discovered by Holmes in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: The Blood Came from the Murderer's Nose

citations.quote: The blood was the murderer's—if it was a murder. I deduced from the height of the writing on the wall... and from the fact that there were no wounds, that the man's nose had bled in his excitement.

nodes.description: Holmes's deduction, from the height of the writing on the wall and the absence of wounds on the victim, that the blood at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene came from the killer's own nose in A Study in Scarlet.
aliases: Blood Came from the Murderer's Nose
normalized_terms: blood came from the murderer's nose
the blood came from the murderer's nose

normalized_terms: ted baldwin's body was misidentified as john douglas

nodes.label: Bunny and Raffles Were School Friends

nodes.description: Bunny and Raffles Were School Friends is the biographical fact established in The Ides of March from The Amateur Cracksman that explains the deep trust and loyalty that draws Bunny into partnership with Raffles.
normalized_terms: bunny and raffles were school friends

nodes.label: Drebber Was Poisoned

citations.quote: Drebber died from poison. The pills which I have here are deadly poison.

nodes.description: The established fact that Enoch Drebber was poisoned, not physically attacked, with the same deadly pills that Jefferson Hope also carried in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Holmes is a Consulting Detective

citations.quote: I'm a consulting detective, if you can understand what that is.

nodes.description: Holmes's own description of himself as the world's only consulting detective, to whom other detectives bring their most difficult cases, first stated in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Holmes Wrote 'The Book of Life'

citations.quote: This article is yours then? Holmes... I cannot deny it.

nodes.description: The fact that Holmes is the anonymous author of the magazine article 'The Book of Life,' which argues that all knowledge of a person can be deduced from observation, established in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Jefferson Hope Has an Aortic Aneurism

citations.quote: It is an aneurism, he said. It comes from over-exposure and under-feeding among the Salt Lake Mountains.

nodes.description: The medical fact, confirmed at Jefferson Hope's examination, that he suffers from a potentially fatal aortic aneurism caused by exposure and hunger in the Salt Lake Mountains, in A Study in Scarlet.
normalized_terms: jefferson hope has an aortic aneurism

nodes.description: The established fact that A.J. Raffles lives in the Albany, Piccadilly, one of London's most prestigious bachelor residences, first stated in The Ides of March.

nodes.description: V.V. 341 = Vermissa Valley Lodge 341 is the fact Holmes deduces in Part I, Chapter VII of The Valley of Fear, decoding the cryptic card found at Birlstone to identify the Scowrer lodge whose members carried out the crime.

nodes.label: Watson Served in Afghanistan
