nodes.label: Murder by Poison Pills

citations.quote: I held the box before him, and told him to choose and abide by his choice. He chose the harmless one.

nodes.description: Murder by Poison Pills is Jefferson Hope's technique in A Study in Scarlet of forcing his victim Enoch Drebber to choose between two pills — one lethal, one harmless — making the death appear to result from chance rather than murder.

nodes.label: The RACHE Misdirection

citations.quote: There is no man living who has worked so much with bloodstains. RACHE is the German for 'revenge'.

nodes.description: The RACHE Misdirection is the false clue Jefferson Hope inscribes in blood on the wall at 3 Lauriston Gardens in A Study in Scarlet, intended to send investigators chasing a German revenge motive rather than the true killer.

nodes.label: String-and-Stone Revolver Experiment

citations.quote: the revolver was tied to the string and the stone, the string drawn across the bridge parapet

nodes.description: The ingenious suicide method in which a revolver tied to a weighted string is fired across a bridge parapet, the recoil flinging the gun into the water and leaving no weapon at the scene in The Problem of Thor Bridge.

nodes.label: Scent Tracking via Stolen Boot

citations.quote: He obtained the old black boot, which he used for laying the scent.
"The boot!" said Holmes eagerly. "It's the scent they needed for the hound."

nodes.description: Scent Tracking via Stolen Boot is Stapleton's technique in The Hound of the Baskervilles of providing his hound with Sir Henry Baskerville's scent by stealing one of the baronet's new boots from his London hotel.

nodes.label: Signal Light to Selden

citations.quote: Barrymore was crouching at the window with the candle held against the glass...stared out into the blackness of the moor.

nodes.description: Barrymore's candle signal at a darkened Baskerville Hall window, used to communicate with Selden the escaped convict hiding on the moor in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Spectroscopic Blood Analysis

citations.section: Chapter III: A Lady in the Case
citations.quote: he replaced one of the objectives by a spectroscopic attachment, and having placed the little lamp close to the microscope mirror, adjusted the spectroscope — fixing the D line in the spectrum

nodes.description: Thorndyke's spectroscopic examination of a blood smear using a micro-attachment to the microscope, providing definitive identification of the substance in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: Typeface / Document Analysis

citations.quote: Holmes pulled out two of the five remaining words, and laid them on the table. They were 'moor' and 'moorland.' 'I could trace every word printed from The Times.'". He then cut the words.

nodes.description: Holmes's technique of cutting words from a newspaper printed in a distinctive typeface to construct an anonymous warning message in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Undercover Detective Work (McMurdo Persona)

citations.quote: Birdy Edwards infiltrates as Jack McMurdo
identity exposed

nodes.description: Undercover Detective Work (McMurdo Persona) is the method Birdy Edwards of Pinkerton's uses in The Valley of Fear, infiltrating the Scowrers under the alias Jack McMurdo and eventually exposing the entire lodge.

nodes.label: Underworld Informant Network

citations.quote: Shinwell Johnson, who is my agent in the lower classes

nodes.description: Shinwell Johnson, Holmes's agent planted in the criminal underworld to gather intelligence on dangerous figures in the lower classes, referenced in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

nodes.label: Voodoo Ritual (Wisteria Lodge)

citations.quote: the voodoo rituals and the fetish at Wisteria Lodge

nodes.description: Voodoo rituals practised at Wisteria Lodge by the cook as part of secret religious observance linked to the household's South American origins in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

nodes.label: Water-Activated Hidden Message Detection

citations.quote: Immediately there began to appear on it a new set of characters of a curious grey colour — Chinese ink surface script concealing waxy pressure-impression revealed when wetted

nodes.description: Thorndyke's technique of wetting a surface script to reveal a hidden Roman-capital message written in water-sensitive ink beneath the visible Chinese ink text in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: X-ray Skiagraph Examination

citations.quote: Thorndyke and Polton set up portable X-ray apparatus at museum. The teeth show a gold crown of modern workmanship — a dental bridge. No priest of Amen Ra would present such a feature.

nodes.description: Thorndyke and Polton's portable X-ray apparatus used to examine a mummy case without opening it, revealing a gold dental crown and positively identifying the body in The Vanishing Man.

nodes.label: Financial Desperation

citations.quote: I was at my wits' end for money

nodes.description: Financial desperation, the motive that drives Bunny Manders to accept Raffles's proposal of burglary when he is at his wits' end for money in The Ides of March.

nodes.label: Laura Lyons's Desire for Divorce and Freedom

citations.quote: He had promised to marry her in case she could get a divorce, and so she wrote the letter to lure Sir Charles to his death.

nodes.description: Laura Lyons's desire for a divorce and remarriage, exploited by Stapleton who promised to wed her if she lured Sir Charles to his death at the Yew Alley gate in The Hound of the Baskervilles.
normalized_terms: laura lyons's desire for divorce and freedom

nodes.label: Inheritance of Baskerville Estate

citations.quote: Stapleton was the son of Rodger Baskerville, the younger brother of Sir Charles, who fled with a sinister reputation to South America, where he was reported to have died unmarried. He had in reality married, and had one child, this fellow, whose real name is the same as his father's.

nodes.description: Inheritance of Baskerville Estate is Jack Stapleton's motive in The Hound of the Baskervilles: as the concealed son of Rodger Baskerville, he plans to kill Sir Charles and Sir Henry to claim the estate as the last surviving heir.

nodes.label: Personal Sense of Justice

citations.quote: Raffles made himself judge and jury

nodes.description: A personal sense of justice, the motive that drives Raffles to act as self-appointed judge and jury in Mr. Justice Raffles.

nodes.label: Revenge for John Ferrier

citations.quote: It was said in the city that the murder of John Ferrier and the abduction of his daughter had been planned by these two.

nodes.description: Revenge for John Ferrier is one of the two motives driving Jefferson Hope's killings in A Study in Scarlet, cited when Holmes explains that the Danite Band's murder of Ferrier was planned by Drebber and Stangerson.

nodes.label: Revenge for Lucy Ferrier

citations.quote: When I saw her in her coffin, I vowed that as I lay dying my last thoughts and my last prayer should be for her death.

nodes.description: Revenge for Lucy Ferrier is Jefferson Hope's primary motive in A Study in Scarlet, his vow sworn over Lucy's coffin that his last thought and last prayer before dying would be for the deaths of Drebber and Stangerson.

nodes.label: Thrill and Sporting Spirit

citations.quote: the sport of it was everything to Raffles

nodes.description: The thrill and sporting spirit of the chase, Raffles's stated primary motive for continuing his career as a gentleman burglar in Le Premier Pas (The Amateur Cracksman).

nodes.label: Antihero (Raffles)

citations.quote: Raffles was at once gentleman and criminal

nodes.description: The antihero role occupied by Raffles, who is simultaneously an admired gentleman and a practised criminal throughout The Amateur Cracksman.

nodes.label: Narrator (Bunny Manders)

citations.quote: I, Manders, tell the tale

nodes.description: The narrator role occupied by Harry 'Bunny' Manders, who tells all the Raffles stories from his position as accomplice and admiring friend in The Amateur Cracksman.

nodes.label: short sabre / cutlass (Admiral's)

citations.quote: he held in his hand a short sabre which was like a navy cutlass, but about twice as big

nodes.description: A short sabre like a navy cutlass but twice the size, carried by the Admiral as his personal weapon in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Agra Treasure

attributes.description: gems: 143 diamonds, 97 emeralds, 170 rubies, pearls, and more
attributes.fate: thrown into Thames by Jonathan Small

citations.quote: the value of the jewels at not less than half a million sterling
The treasure is lost, said Miss Morstan, calmly
one hundred and forty-three diamonds of the first water

nodes.description: The Agra Treasure, a chest containing over one hundred and forty-three diamonds of the first water and other jewels valued at no less than half a million sterling in The Sign of the Four, lost to the Thames when Jonathan Small jettisoned it.

nodes.label: Von Herder Air Gun

citations.section: The Adventure of the Empty House
citations.quote: a sort of gun, with a curiously misshapen butt

nodes.description: The Von Herder air gun, a custom-built silent weapon with a curiously misshapen butt commissioned by Moriarty and used by Colonel Moran in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: The Aluminium Dagger (TRADITORE)

citations.quote: 'TRADITORE' — word engraved on aluminium dagger guard; slender, aluminium hilt, bookbinder's morocco covering; blade from 3/4 inch common tool steel; designed to fit Chassepôt barrel

nodes.description: An aluminium-hilted Mycenaean-style dagger with the word TRADITORE engraved on its guard and a blade of old foreign steel, the murder weapon in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Yellow dressing-gown with scarlet lining

citations.quote: the body of an old man in a yellow dressing-gown with a very vivid scarlet lining

nodes.description: The yellow dressing-gown with a vivid scarlet lining found on the body of old Sir Aaron Armstrong at the opening of The Hammer of God in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Horn-hilted knife (red-smeared)

citations.quote: He took from his tail-pocket a long horn-hilted knife with a red smear on it, and handed it politely to the sergeant.

nodes.description: A horn-hilted knife with a red smear, produced by Father Brown and handed to the police sergeant during the investigation in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Rope (found on corpse)

citations.quote: A scrap of rope seemed caught about his leg, entangled presumably in a struggle.

nodes.description: A scrap of rope found entangled on the corpse in the Quinton garden, apparently caught in a struggle in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Australian boomerang (war-club)

citations.quote: there is only one kind of club that can be thrown out of a window, turn in mid-air and come back to the window next to it: the Australian boomerang. You'll see some of them in the Major's study.

nodes.description: An Australian boomerang used as a war-club, identified by Father Brown as the only weapon that can be thrown from a window, curve in the air, and return, in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Loaded automatic pistol

citations.quote: the loaded automatic pistol that he found in a drawer of the library desk

nodes.description: A loaded automatic pistol found by Father Brown in a library desk drawer and used during the climax of a case in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Ted Baldwin's Disguise Clothes

citations.quote: found in moat bundle, explains body substitution

nodes.description: Ted Baldwin's disguise clothes, recovered from the moat at Birlstone Manor, which explain how the body was misidentified and John Douglas faked his own death in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: Bamboo walking stick

citations.quote: A senseless stick of bamboo, with the top rather splintered.

nodes.description: A senseless stick of bamboo with a splintered top, found near a crime scene and examined for clues in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Razor (barber's)

citations.quote: In that moment of time he perceived in the inner room the razor you had just laid down, and the yellow-white head of Sir Arthur in the barber's chair... he picked up the razor and cut the throat.

nodes.description: The barber's razor laid down by Wicks just before Dalmon used it to cut Vaudrey's throat in the barber's chair in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Baskerville Manuscript (1742)

citations.quote: "I have in my pocket a manuscript," said Dr. James Mortimer. "I observed it as you entered the room," said Holmes. "It is an old manuscript." "Early eighteenth century, unless it is a forgery."
The manuscript is short and is intimately connected with the affair.

nodes.description: The 1742 Baskerville family manuscript delivered by Dr. Mortimer, which recounts the curse of Hugo Baskerville and provides historical context for the case in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Two crossed battle-axes

citations.section: The Worst Crime in the World
citations.quote: There were two crossed battle-axes hung on one side of the fireplace, two crossed battle-axes on the other.

nodes.description: Two pairs of crossed battle-axes hung on the walls of the Musgrave Castle fireplace, part of the ancestral arms decorating the hall in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Beehives

citations.quote: In the background was a row of sunny beehives, along which an elderly man, presumably Mr. Smith, was moving.

nodes.description: A row of sunny beehives at the back of the garden, tended by Mr. Smith the supposed beekeeper who introduces himself to Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: John Bellingham's Egyptian Scarab

citations.quote: a large blue-glazed Egyptian scarab; John Bellingham always carries it on his person — planted by Jellicoe to create confusion

nodes.description: John Bellingham's large blue-glazed Egyptian scarab, which he always carried on his person and which was planted by Jellicoe to create confusion about the identification of remains in The Vanishing Man.

nodes.label: Beryl Coronet

citations.section: XI. The Adventure of the Beryl Coronet
citations.quote: a beryl coronet, one of the most precious public possessions of the Empire... thirty-nine enormous beryls

nodes.description: The Beryl Coronet, one of the most precious public possessions of the Empire, set with thirty-nine enormous beryls, stolen and then recovered by Holmes in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: big box (in corner)

citations.quote: the Other Man (or whatever it is) crawls out every night from the big box in the corner, which is kept locked all day

nodes.description: A big locked box in the corner of a room, from which something emerges each night according to a strange report in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Black hood/mask (worn by Maurice pretending to be James)

citations.quote: He was looking at the ground like a monk, and hidden in a black hood that was really as ridiculous as any mask.

nodes.description: A black hood worn by Maurice Mair to resemble his brother James as a penitent monk, a disguise that conceals his identity during the long impersonation in The Secret of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: black hood/mask (worn by maurice pretending to be james)

nodes.label: The Black Pearl

citations.quote: Victor Danègre had stolen the black pearl from the Countess Zalti's apartment.

nodes.description: The Black Pearl, a jewel stolen from the Countess Zalti's apartment by Victor Danègre and subsequently recovered by Arsène Lupin in The Extraordinary Adventures of Arsène Lupin.

nodes.label: Black Pearl of the Borgias

citations.section: The Adventure of the Six Napoleons
citations.quote: one of the most precious stones in existence — the black Pearl of the Borgias

nodes.description: The Black Pearl of the Borgias, one of the most precious stones in existence, stolen from the Countess of Morcar and recovered by Holmes in The Return of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: Blickensderfer Typewriter (with broken 'e')

citations.quote: Label typed on a second-hand Blickensderfer literary typewheel — characteristic break in the loop of the small 'e' identifies Walter Hornby as the criminal

nodes.description: A second-hand Blickensderfer typewriter with a distinctive broken loop on the small letter 'e', whose characteristic defect allows Thorndyke to trace a forged label in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: The Bloody Thumb (penny dreadful book Boulnois reading)

citations.section: The Strange Crime of John Boulnois
citations.quote: I was reading 'The Bloody Thumb.'

nodes.description: The Bloody Thumb, a penny dreadful that John Boulnois was reading at home on the very evening Sir Claude Champion was killed in The Strange Crime of John Boulnois (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
aliases: Bloody Thumb (penny dreadful book Boulnois reading)
normalized_terms: bloody thumb (penny dreadful book boulnois reading)
the bloody thumb (penny dreadful book boulnois reading)

nodes.label: Blowpipe

citations.quote: diminutive footmarks, naked feet, stone-headed wooden mace, small poisoned darts
he plucked out a short, round piece of wood, like a school-ruler, and clapped it to his lips

nodes.description: The Blowpipe is Tonga's weapon in The Sign of the Four, a short round piece of wood clapped to his lips to propel the poison thorn that kills Bartholomew Sholto through his locked window.

nodes.label: Blue Carbuncle Gemstone

citations.section: VII. The Adventure of the Blue Carbuncle
citations.quote: a brilliantly scintillating blue stone, rather smaller than a bean in size, but of the most intense and perfect blue in colour—the Countess of Morcar's blue carbuncle

nodes.description: The Blue Carbuncle, an intensely blue gemstone smaller than a bean, found inside a Christmas goose crop and traced by Holmes to its rightful owner in The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: Blue Cross (sapphire cross)

citations.quote: a Father Brown from Essex was bringing up a silver cross with sapphires, a relic of considerable value

nodes.description: The Blue Cross, a silver reliquary cross set with sapphires and considerable relic value, carried by Father Brown from Essex to a London conference and stolen then recovered from Flambeau in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Blue curtain (used as turban by Jameson)

citations.section: The Song of the Flying Fish
citations.quote: the length of old blue curtain that he wrapped round his head

nodes.description: A length of old blue curtain wrapped round Jameson's head as an improvised turban for his disguise during the events in The Secret of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: blue curtain (used as turban by jameson)

nodes.label: The Blue Diamond

citations.section: First Episode: The Blonde Lady, Chapter II: The Blue Diamond
citations.quote: Ganimard worked tirelessly to recover the Blue Diamond stolen by Lupin's blonde accomplice.

nodes.description: The Blue Diamond, the jewel stolen by Lupin's blonde accomplice that Ganimard strives to recover in The Blonde Lady (Arsène Lupin).

nodes.label: blue spectacles (Hirsch/Dubosc's disguise)

citations.quote: he--was clean-shaven and very pale... he wore blue spectacles

nodes.description: Blue spectacles worn by the clean-shaven Colonel Dubosc as part of his disguise when impersonating Dr. Hirsch in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Japanese/Chinese helmet (Colonel Bohun's hat)

citations.quote: he took off the queer round hat covered with green, showing that it was lined within with steel. Wilfred recognised it indeed as a light Japanese or Chinese helmet torn down from a trophy that hung in the old family hall.

nodes.description: Colonel Norman Bohun's round hat lined with steel, originally a Japanese or Chinese helmet that Wilfred recognises at the crime scene in The Hammer of God (The Innocence of Father Brown).

nodes.label: The 'Book of Life' Article

citations.quote: Its somewhat ambitious title was 'The Book of Life,' and it attempted to show how much an observant man might learn.

nodes.description: The 'Book of Life' Article is Holmes's essay with the ambitious title arguing that an observant man can learn a great deal from a brief encounter, first discussed in Part I, Chapter II of A Study in Scarlet when Watson reads it in a magazine.

nodes.label: brass telescope on tripod

citations.quote: in a treeless space on one side of the house opening upon the river stood a tall brass tripod on which was tilted a big brass telescope

nodes.description: A large brass telescope on a tall tripod tilted towards the river, set up by the Admiral outside the tower for watching for ships in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: brigands' carbines

citations.quote: he saw, looking down on them, the muzzles of four other carbines and four other brown faces

nodes.description: The carbines of the four brigands who cover the coach party from a high ridge, used to enforce the ransom demand in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Broken looking-glass (mirror)

citations.quote: A big looking-glass broken, and a small palm tree knocked over, and the pot smashed all over the floor.

nodes.description: A big looking-glass smashed during the violent struggle in the room, found alongside an overturned palm tree in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: bronze coin with head of Caesar

citations.quote: It was a bronze coin, and the colour, combined with the exact curve of the Roman nose... made the head of Caesar on it the almost precise portrait of Philip Hawker.

nodes.description: A bronze Roman coin bearing the profile of Caesar, whose colour and exact curve of the nose make it the object of Christabel's sentimental theft in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: bronze coin with head of caesar

nodes.label: bronze lectern / Bible stand (in hermitage)

citations.section: The Fairy Tale of Father Brown
citations.quote: On the small rock platform stood an old bronze lectern or reading-stand, groaning under a great German Bible.

nodes.description: An old bronze lectern on a small rock platform in the hermitage, bearing a great German Bible, in The Fairy Tale of Father Brown (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Brown paper parcels

citations.quote: he had several brown paper parcels, which he was quite incapable of collecting

nodes.description: Father Brown's several brown paper parcels, which he is quite incapable of keeping together, referenced in The Blue Cross in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: brown-paper parcels (modern English coins hidden in)

citations.quote: out of which poured and rolled, not Roman, but very modern English coins

nodes.description: Brown-paper parcels concealing modern English coins rather than ancient Roman ones, part of the Glengyle gold mania plot in The Innocence of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: brown-paper parcels (modern english coins hidden in)

nodes.label: Bruce-Partington Submarine Plans

citations.section: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans
citations.quote: the Bruce-Partington submarine plans

nodes.description: The Bruce-Partington submarine plans, top-secret government documents stolen from Woolwich Arsenal and used as bait for a spy ring in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.

nodes.label: bullet (that killed Prince Otto)

citations.section: The Fairy Tale of Father Brown
citations.quote: What happened was this... the blood still pulsed from his shattered temple and jaw... He was shot by his own orders.

nodes.description: The bullet that killed Prince Otto of Grossenmark, fired by his own sentries on forged orders from Colonel Dubosc, shattering his temple and jaw in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Cardboard Box with Two Ears

citations.section: II. The Adventure of the Cardboard Box
citations.quote: a small cardboard box which contained two human ears, two freshly severed human ears

nodes.description: A cardboard box containing two freshly severed human ears sent anonymously to a woman in Croydon, the shocking opening clue in The Adventure of the Cardboard Box (Sherlock Holmes).

nodes.label: Carstairs Collection of Roman coins

citations.quote: my father was that Colonel Carstairs you've probably heard of, who made the famous Carstairs Collection of Roman coins

nodes.description: The famous Carstairs Collection of Roman coins assembled by Colonel Carstairs, inherited and then endangered by his sons in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: carved symbols on doorbeam (tower, ship, half-man)

citations.quote: three carved symbols, which, though very worn and obscure, seemed still to convey some sense to him... the legend of the Spaniard plain enough.

nodes.description: Three worn carved symbols on the Pendragon tower doorbeam — a tower, a ship, and a half-man — depicting the legend of the Spanish curse in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: carved symbols on doorbeam (tower, ship, half-man)

nodes.label: Cavalry sabre (O'Brien's / murder weapon)

citations.quote: reappeared with a flashing naked cavalry sabre, streaked with blood about the point and edge

nodes.description: A flashing naked cavalry sabre streaked with blood at the point, produced by O'Brien during the confrontation in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Chablis and almonds (on table outside hotel)

citations.quote: a small round restaurant table, and on this stood a small bottle of Chablis and a plate of almonds and raisins

nodes.description: A small bottle of Chablis and a plate of almonds and raisins set on a round table outside a hotel, observed by Father Brown as a significant clue in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: chablis and almonds (on table outside hotel)

nodes.label: Chest of Silver

citations.quote: the great chest of silver plate

nodes.description: The great chest of silver plate, the object at the centre of the crime in The Chest of Silver (Raffles: A Thief in the Night).

nodes.label: Chloroform

citations.quote: 'Chloroform,' he said as he rose; 'I only guessed it just now.'

nodes.description: Chloroform, identified by Father Brown as the means by which Leonard Quinton was rendered unconscious before being murdered in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: churchwarden pipes and wine glasses

citations.quote: what gave me my first old-fashioned impression was simply a set of tall, old-fashioned wine-glasses, one or two lemons and two churchwarden pipes

nodes.description: Tall old-fashioned wine glasses and churchwarden pipes, the old-fashioned drinking paraphernalia that gives Father Brown an initial impression in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Porlock's Cipher Message

citations.quote: It is Porlock's writing

nodes.description: Porlock's Cipher Message is the encoded letter sent to Holmes by the informer Fred Porlock in The Valley of Fear, warning of danger at Birlstone and decoded by Holmes using Whitaker's Almanac.

nodes.label: Cocaine Bottle

attributes.significance: symbol of Holmes's boredom and drug use

citations.quote: It is cocaine, a seven-per-cent. solution. Would you care to try it?
there still remains the cocaine-bottle, and he stretched his long white hand up

nodes.description: The cocaine bottle kept by Holmes for his seven-per-cent solution, a recurring symbol of his restless and self-destructive mental habits first mentioned in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Colonel Adams's sword (over the fireplace)

citations.quote: In the large entrance hall of the house… In front of the large hall fire, over which hung the colonel's sword

nodes.description: Colonel Adams's sword hung over the fireplace in his entrance hall, a domestic trophy marking his military career in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Coloured lamps / garden illumination

citations.quote: he preferred to do it when he's alone. He likes playing with a little plant of electricity that he works from that bungalow or hut over there

nodes.description: Coloured electric lamps strung in the garden, powered by a small plant of electricity from the bungalow, used for garden illumination in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: conjuring bill / handbill

citations.quote: he produced from his pocket a big bill printed in blue and red

nodes.description: A conjuring handbill printed in blue and red advertising a performance, produced from a pocket during a case in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Prescott's Counterfeiting Press

citations.quote: the counterfeiting press hidden beneath the floor

nodes.description: Prescott's counterfeiting press, hidden beneath the floor of Nathan Garrideb's house and the true target of the criminal scheme in The Adventure of the Three Garridebs.

nodes.label: service revolver (Cray's)

citations.quote: the figure was much longer, leaner, and more athletic... Father Brown only saw one thing about the man; which was the revolver in his hand.

nodes.description: Colonel Cray's service revolver, which the long, lean, athletic figure of Cray is seen brandishing when Father Brown first encounters him in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Crookes Tube (X-ray apparatus)

citations.quote: Thorndyke and Polton set up portable X-ray apparatus at museum. Skiagraph taken of mummy case without opening — reveals modern dental bridgework

nodes.description: Thorndyke and Polton's portable Crookes tube X-ray apparatus, used to take a skiagraph of a sealed mummy case at the museum in The Vanishing Man.

nodes.label: Crown of Charles I (hidden treasure)

citations.quote: a little coronet... a coronal of gold... It was indeed the missing crowne of Charles I

nodes.description: The missing Crown of Charles I, a small golden coronal recovered from its hiding place by Holmes in The Musgrave Ritual in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: cruet-stand (hidden in dustbin, with emetic contents)

citations.quote: He threw the cruet in the dustbin--where I found it, along with other silver--for the sake of a burglary blind.

nodes.description: The silver cruet-stand concealed in a dustbin as part of a staged burglary blind, recovered by Father Brown to expose a poison plot in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: The Crystal Stopper

citations.quote: a little crystal stopper, like those used for the bottles in a liqueur-stand — the crystal stopper had disappeared.

citations.section: Chapter VII: The Profile of Napoleon

citations.quote: Inside the stopper Lupin found the Cross of Lorraine — the key to everything.

nodes.description: Curare-tipped arrows brought from South America by Mrs. Ferguson, one of which is used to poison the baby, the key evidence in The Adventure of the Sussex Vampire.

nodes.description: A Cyanea capillata, the Lion's Mane jellyfish of extraordinary size whose sting causes the horrifying death that mystifies Holmes in The Adventure of the Lion's Mane.

nodes.description: Cigarettes concealing two potassium cyanide tabloids a quarter-inch from the end, with gold tips and loose wrappers, used as a means of murder in The Vanishing Man.

nodes.label: dagger-sword

citations.quote: what looked like a long knife or short sword, straight, but with an ornamental and pictured handle

nodes.description: A straight, long knife or short sword with an ornamental pictured handle, wielded in a menacing confrontation in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: decanter (smashed on Green's head)

nodes.description: A decanter smashed in dead silence onto the bald head of Isaac Green by the Duke, a sudden violent act of justice in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Deep-Sea Sand (Eastern Mediterranean)

citations.section: A Message from the Deep Sea
citations.quote: It is a message to us from the deep sea, Jervis; from the floor of the Eastern Mediterranean — sand found on Minna's pillow; Foraminifera identifying Turkey sponge warehouse

nodes.description: A sample of Eastern Mediterranean deep-sea sand bearing Levant-specific Foraminifera, found on a dead woman's boot and used by Thorndyke to trace her last voyage in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Defaced Catholic missals (gold removed)

citations.quote: a few old missals and little Catholic pictures, which the Ogilvies kept… curiously cut about and defaced

nodes.description: Old Catholic missals curiously cut about and defaced, stripped of their gilt and ornamental gold by the obsessive Lord Glengyle in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Devil's Foot Root (Radix pedis diaboli)

citations.section: The Adventure of the Devil's Foot
citations.quote: radix pedis diaboli — the Devil's Foot root

nodes.description: Devil's Foot root (Radix pedis diaboli), a rare and deadly West African poison that causes madness and death when burned, central to The Adventure of the Devil's Foot.

nodes.label: Douglas Maberley's Novel Manuscript

citations.quote: a novel written by Douglas — a novel which must not be published

nodes.description: Douglas Maberley's unpublished novel manuscript, which contains compromising revelations about Isadora Klein and must be suppressed, driving the plot of The Adventure of the Three Gables.

nodes.label: Pistol (duel weapon)

citations.quote: he was not so good a shot, but there was no question of missing the heart at that distance... Maurice's pistol still lay under his hand and it was undischarged. Then as the elder man bent over the younger, the younger lifted himself on his left arm and shot the elder through the body.

nodes.description: The pistol used in the staged duel between Maurice and James Mair, which Maurice kept and fired at point-blank range as James bent over the pretended fallen body in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Dumbbell / Bundle

citations.quote: Holmes suspects bundle in moat
Barker lifts bundle containing Baldwin's clothes from moat

nodes.description: A bundle concealing Ted Baldwin's clothing, sunk in the Birlstone Manor moat by Barker and recovered by Holmes, proving the body-substitution scheme in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: The Dynamics of an Asteroid (Moriarty's Book)

citations.quote: The Dynamics of an Asteroid

nodes.description: The Dynamics of an Asteroid, a mathematics treatise of such ascent of thought that no scientific journal would review it, authored by Professor Moriarty, mentioned in The Valley of Fear.
normalized_terms: the dynamics of an asteroid (moriarty's book)

nodes.label: electric torch (Kidd's)

citations.section: The Strange Crime of John Boulnois
citations.quote: The Yankee journalist took an electric torch from his pocket

nodes.description: An electric torch produced from his pocket by the American journalist Kidd to illuminate the dark Pendragon tower in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Emerald necklace (Mrs. Bankes)

citations.quote: she even possessed a very valuable emerald necklace upstairs, that gave her a right to prominence in a discussion about thieves.

nodes.description: Mrs. Bankes's valuable emerald necklace, discussed openly at dinner as a precious possession before it is stolen during the night's events in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Fountain pen (emptied by Joan)

citations.quote: her fountain pens were carefully filled by her sister--all except this fountain pen. This was carefully not filled by her sister

nodes.description: Father Brown's fountain pen deliberately left unfilled by his sister Joan Stacey so that Pauline could not complete the will she was writing before her death in The Eye of Apollo (The Innocence of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Fallen palm tree in pot

citations.quote: A tall palm, with long sweeping leaves, had fallen full length, and its dark red pot was shattered into shards.

nodes.description: A tall palm tree with long sweeping leaves fallen full length in its shattered dark red pot, part of the room wreckage found in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: false/crooked nose (disguise wax prosthetic)

citations.quote: The wax always spots like that just a little in this weather. But it's such a crooked nose.

nodes.description: A wax prosthetic nose forming part of a disguise, observed beginning to spot and warp in warm weather, betraying the actor Hugo Romaine in The Dagger with Wings (The Secret of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Family portraits (gallery)

citations.section: The Worst Crime in the World
citations.quote: the dark colours of the rows of family portraits

nodes.description: Rows of family portraits in dark colours lining the gallery walls of Musgrave Castle in The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Umbrella (Father Brown's)

citations.quote: He had a large, shabby umbrella, which constantly fell on the floor.
He carried a short thick umbrella with a knob like a club, at the sight of which his Latin friend almost shed tears of sentiment.

nodes.description: Father Brown's large shabby umbrella, constantly falling on the floor, his characteristic accessory that appears throughout The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: finger-prints on sword (half-way down blade)

citations.section: The Strange Crime of John Boulnois
citations.quote: There were finger-prints on that sword... They were half-way down the blade of the sword. Whose prints they were I have no earthly clue; but why should anybody hold a sword half-way down?

nodes.description: Fingerprints found half-way down the blade of a sword, indicating an unusual grip that points to how the murder of Aurora Rome was committed in The Man in the Passage (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Five-Clawed Club (Murder Weapon)

citations.quote: a specially-constructed club with five sharp points on it, mimicking a lion's claw

nodes.description: A specially constructed club with five sharp points mimicking a lion's claw, the true murder weapon disguised as an animal mauling in The Adventure of the Veiled Lodger.

nodes.label: Five Dried Orange Pips

citations.quote: "K. K. K.," and then "put the papers on the sundial," he said as he tore open the envelope and there fell out of it five little dried orange pips.

nodes.description: Five dried orange pips sent in an envelope by the Ku Klux Klan as a death warning to Openshaw in The Five Orange Pips (The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes).

nodes.label: Flambeau's children (heard laughing above)

citations.quote: the belated laughter of Flambeau's children in the high, dark house above them

nodes.description: Flambeau's unseen children whose belated laughter is heard from the high dark castle above where Father Brown and Flambeau sit talking in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: flat shining dagger (dropped by hotel proprietor)

citations.quote: the stranger quail and dodge, and then dart into his inn, leaving the flat and shining dagger he had dropped exactly where it had fallen.

nodes.description: A flat shining dagger dropped by the fleeing hotel proprietor on the Seawood esplanade, the intended murder weapon in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
normalized_terms: flat shining dagger (dropped by hotel proprietor)

nodes.label: The Flying Stars (three African diamonds)

citations.quote: In a nest of orange velvet lay like three eggs, three white and vivid diamonds… 'They're the three great African diamonds called 'The Flying Stars,' because they've been stolen so often.'

nodes.description: The three Flying Stars, great African diamonds displayed in a nest of orange velvet as a Christmas present, stolen by Flambeau in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: secret note / forged paper (noiseless powder formula direction)

citations.quote: Tell the man the formula for powder is in grey envelope in first drawer to the left of Secretary's desk, War Office, in red ink. He must be careful. P.H.

nodes.description: A forged spy's note directing an agent to a grey envelope in the War Office Secretary's desk, used to frame Dr. Hirsch for treason in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
normalized_terms: secret note / forged paper (noiseless powder formula direction)

nodes.label: garden hose (used to fight fire)

citations.quote: He did not find a spud; but he found an old broom made of twigs... and now let's go and water the flowers. He turned the little tap on the instrument, and the water shot out straight and solid as a long rod of steel.

nodes.description: A garden hose wielded by Father Brown to fight the fire burning in the Pendragon wooden tower in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: 24 Chromicized Gelatine Stamps (fingerprint forgery tools)

citations.section: Chapter XVI: Thorndyke Plays His Card
citations.quote: By means of a stamp, either of indiarubber or, more probably, of chromicized gelatine — Thorndyke produces 24 boxwood-handled stamps for court demonstration

nodes.description: Twenty-four chromicized gelatine stamps on boxwood handles produced by Thorndyke to demonstrate how a fingerprint forgery can be manufactured in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: Giant gilt eye (symbol of Apollo cult)

citations.quote: It was an enormous gilt effigy of the human eye, surrounded with rays of gold, and taking up as much room as two or three of the office windows.

nodes.description: An enormous gilt effigy of a human eye surrounded by rays of gold, occupying the space of two or three windows in the Eye of Apollo cult offices in The Innocence of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: giant gilt eye (symbol of apollo cult)

nodes.label: Gold (collection mania)

citations.quote: Diamonds without their gold rings; candles without their gold candlesticks; snuff without the gold snuff-boxes; pencil-leads without the gold pencil-cases

nodes.description: The accumulated gold objects stripped of their settings by Lord Glengyle — rings, candlesticks, snuff-boxes, pencil-cases — as part of his gold collection mania in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Skull of Lord Glengyle

citations.quote: something that did not look like a potato, but rather like a monstrous, over-domed mushroom. But it struck the spade with a cold click; it rolled over like a ball, and grinned up at them. 'The Earl of Glengyle,' said Brown sadly

nodes.description: The skull of Lord Glengyle, exhumed from its grave in the churchyard and kept by the family in their castle as part of their eccentric gold-hoarding obsession in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: gold pin with monkey head (murder weapon)

citations.quote: It was a very long gold pin, and had the carved head of a monkey or some such thing, and it was fixed in a rather odd way--he wore pince-nez and a broad black--... his pince-nez was broken under him, and the long gold pin wasn't stuck through his purple scarf but through his heart.

nodes.description: A very long gold pin with a carved monkey head, thrust through a man's heart while appearing to be merely fixed in his scarf, the murder weapon in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
normalized_terms: gold pin with monkey head (murder weapon)

nodes.label: Golden Pince-Nez Spectacles

citations.section: The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez
citations.quote: a pair of golden pince-nez spectacles clutched in the dead man's hand

nodes.description: A pair of golden pince-nez spectacles clutched in the dead man's hand, the sole clue that leads Holmes to solve the mystery in The Adventure of the Golden Pince-Nez (The Return of Sherlock Holmes).

nodes.label: Goldfish bowl (Venetian glass with gold fish / ruby eyes)

citations.section: The Song of the Flying Fish
citations.quote: a huge bubble of beautifully blown Venetian glass, very thin and delicately clouded with faintly iridescent colour, in the tinted twilight of which hung grotesque golden fishes with great rubies for eyes.

nodes.description: A huge bubble of beautifully blown Venetian glass housing gold fish with ruby eyes, apparently exploded like a bomb during a robbery in The Secret of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: goldfish bowl (venetian glass with gold fish / ruby eyes)

nodes.label: Gramophone

citations.quote: a gramophone which would deceive them entirely

nodes.description: A gramophone placed in an adjacent room to simulate Holmes's voice in conversation, enabling him to move undetected while Count Sylvius incriminated himself in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone.

nodes.label: ancient Greek Mycenaean bronze dagger

citations.quote: it was an ancient Greek dagger of the Mycenaean Epoch... It was made of brass like all the Heroic weapons, but, oddly enough, sharp enough to prick anyone still.

nodes.description: An ancient Greek Mycenaean bronze dagger of the Heroic epoch with a blade still sharp enough to cut in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Green evening coats

citations.quote: you will observe, as he takes off his overcoat, that his evening coat is green and not black

nodes.description: Green evening coats worn by the Twelve True Fishermen as the distinctive insignia of their exclusive dining club in The Queer Feet (The Innocence of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Dark green idol (the god)

citations.quote: high above these, its outline dark against the dying light, was a great green image. Its back was turned to them and its face so completely invisible in the hunched posture that the statue might almost have been headless.

nodes.description: A great dark green idol with its back turned, outlined against the dying light at the top of a room in Lord Mounteagle's Asian museum in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Baron Gruner's Brown Leather Diary

citations.quote: the brown leather book — a diary of sorts

nodes.description: Baron Gruner's brown leather diary of his past conquests, stolen by Holmes for Violet de Merville to read as proof of Gruner's true character in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client.

nodes.label: The Haemoglobin Reagent

citations.quote: I've found it! I've found it. I have found a re-agent which is precipitated by haemoglobin, and by nothing else.

nodes.description: The Haemoglobin Reagent is the chemical test Holmes excitedly demonstrates at St. Bartholomew's at the opening of A Study in Scarlet, a reagent precipitated by haemoglobin and nothing else, revolutionising the detection of bloodstains.

nodes.label: Harlequin costume of silver paper

citations.quote: the harlequin, already clad in silver paper out of cigar boxes

nodes.description: A harlequin costume made from silver paper out of cigar boxes, worn by Flambeau in his disguise during the Christmas gathering in The Flying Stars (The Innocence of Father Brown).

nodes.label: wild flowers (Hedwig's, short-stalked)

citations.section: The Fairy Tale of Father Brown
citations.quote: Some girl attached to the Court named Hedwig... had been out in the wood picking wild flowers. The flowers were quite short, plucked close under the head.

nodes.description: Short-stalked wild flowers picked by a girl named Hedwig from the wood near Heiligwaldenstein castle, whose short stalks become a significant clue in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: legendary gold of Heiligwaldenstein

citations.section: The Fairy Tale of Father Brown
citations.quote: the old question of the alleged supply of gold from the rocks round here, upon which the small city-state had so long maintained its credit

nodes.description: The legendary gold supposedly hidden in the rocks around Heiligwaldenstein, the prize that drove Prince Otto to invade the toy kingdom in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: The Hollow Needle

citations.quote: Beautrelet found the entrance to the Hollow Needle in the sea cliff at Étretat.

citations.section: Chapter Seven: The Treatise of the Needle
citations.quote: The problem of the Hollow Needle it was called, by the countless solvers of the puzzle.
Making 'hollow needle'! — Beautrelet deciphered the parchment and found the words.

citations.section: Chapter Ten: The Treasures of the Kings of France
citations.quote: The Hollow Needle contained the treasures of the Kings of France, accumulated over centuries.

nodes.description: The Hollow Needle, a sea-rock off the Étretat cliffs concealing the secret treasure-chamber of the Kings of France, the prize at the centre of The Hollow Needle (Arsène Lupin).

nodes.label: Holmes's Violin

citations.quote: He would close his eyes and scrape carelessly at the fiddle which was thrown across his knee.

nodes.description: Holmes's violin, played carelessly but brilliantly in long nocturnal sessions as a stimulus for thought or a relief from boredom, first described in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Horn-rimmed spectacles (disguise)

citations.quote: Beside it lay an old pair of heavy horn-rimmed spectacles.

nodes.description: A pair of old heavy horn-rimmed spectacles found beside a body in The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Hornby Diamonds (stolen rough diamonds)

citations.quote: it appeared that the parcel was an unusually large one and contained stones of exceptional size and value. Under these circumstances Mr. Reuben was sent down to the docks

nodes.description: The Hornby rough diamonds, an unusually large parcel of exceptional-value stones stolen from St. Mary Axe, whose theft triggers the wrongful prosecution in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: Tommy Hunter's glove (painted brown)

citations.quote: I think the hand you saw was Hunter's hand, painted brown... He didn't want to take his glove off, because his hand was painted pale brown already.

nodes.description: Tommy Hunter's glove concealing a hand painted brown to pass as a dark-skinned hand and deceive the witnesses in The Red Moon of Meru (The Secret of Father Brown).

nodes.label: poison (Indian, reversible by emetics)

citations.quote: That book on his table was a work on poisons; and was put down open at the place where it stated that a certain Indian poison, though deadly and difficult to trace, was particularly easily reversible by the use of the commonest emetics.

nodes.description: An Indian poison reversible by emetics, described in a book on poisons on Major Putnam's table and used in the plot to kill Colonel Cray in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: iron garden seat (used by Flambeau as weapon)

citations.quote: Flambeau had no weapon, but his large brown hands were resting on the end of the long iron seat. His shoulders abruptly altered their shape, and he heaved the whole huge thing high over his head

nodes.description: A long iron garden seat used by Flambeau as a weapon when he abruptly heaves it at opponents in The God of the Gongs (The Wisdom of Father Brown).
normalized_terms: iron garden seat (used by flambeau as weapon)

nodes.label: Iron Treasure Chest

attributes.description: solid iron chest of Indian workmanship

citations.quote: A solid iron chest of Indian workmanship stood upon the deck
We both stood gazing in astonishment. The box was empty!

nodes.description: The iron treasure chest of Indian workmanship opened aboard ship to reveal the Agra treasure — only to be found empty when the seal was broken in The Sign of the Four.

nodes.label: Two Italian rapiers

citations.quote: He took out of it two long Italian rapiers, with splendid steel hilts and blades, which he planted point downwards in the lawn.

nodes.description: Two long Italian rapiers with splendid steel hilts, produced by Flambeau for the Christmas charade fencing game in The Flying Stars (The Innocence of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Ivory Box with Poison Spring

citations.section: The Adventure of the Dying Detective
citations.quote: a spring within the ivory box which pricked the finger

nodes.description: An ivory box fitted with a concealed spring that pricks the opener's finger with a poisoned needle, sent to Holmes as an assassination device in The Adventure of the Dying Detective.

nodes.label: The Jewish Lamp

citations.section: Second Episode: The Jewish Lamp, Chapter I
citations.quote: M. d'Imblevalle had summoned Holmlock Shears to recover the stolen Jewish lamp.

citations.section: Second Episode: The Jewish Lamp, Chapter II
citations.quote: The Jewish Lamp was Lupin's target, not merely a cover for the real theft.

nodes.description: The Jewish lamp stolen from M. d'Imblevalle's home on the Rue Murillo, apparently the sole target but actually a decoy for Lupin's real theft in The Blonde Lady.

nodes.label: 'A British Officer in Burmah and Brazil' (book by Lt-Col Keith)

citations.section: The Sign of the Broken Sword
citations.quote: this man, then Lieutenant-Colonel Keith, published a sort of autobiography called 'A British Officer in Burmah and Brazil.'

nodes.description: A British Officer in Burmah and Brazil, a sort of autobiography published by Lieutenant-Colonel Keith, used by Father Brown to trace the truth about General St. Clare in The Innocence of Father Brown.
aliases: 'A British Officer in Burmah and Brazil'
normalized_terms: 'a british officer in burmah and brazil'
'a british officer in burmah and brazil' (book by lt-col keith)

nodes.label: Lady Pulman's tiara

citations.quote: Lady Pulman's jewels have gone

nodes.description: Lady Pulman's jewelled tiara and other jewels, stolen in the Beechwood House burglary investigated in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Langur Monkey Serum

citations.quote: a serum obtained from the langur monkey — a terrible experiment in rejuvenation

nodes.description: Langur Monkey Serum is the experimental rejuvenation substance derived from a langur monkey, obtained by Dr. H. Lowenstein and secretly self-administered by Professor Presbury, causing his ape-like nocturnal behaviour in The Adventure of the Creeping Man.

nodes.label: Lift shaft (murder weapon)

citations.quote: Kalon brought the lift to the girl's landing… Then he pressed a button and shot soundlessly up to his own floor… when the poor girl, having finished her work, ran gaily out to where lover and lift were to receive her, and stepped--

nodes.description: The lift shaft in the Eye of Apollo building, used by Kalon to murder Pauline Stacey by causing her to step into an empty shaft in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Light brown sack

citations.quote: 'Stupid of me! I forgot to ask the policeman something. I wonder if they found a light brown sack.'

nodes.description: A light brown sack inquired after by Father Brown of the police, significant to identifying the means by which a body was transported in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: lilies-of-the-valley (Cutler's flowers)

citations.quote: He was still grotesquely grasping a bunch of lilies-of-the-valley.

nodes.description: A bunch of lilies-of-the-valley grotesquely still clutched by Captain Cutler after the violent struggle in The Man in the Passage (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: L.L. Letter to Sir Charles

citations.quote: The name Laura Lyons was on the note-paper—L.L.
I had just found that the letter signed 'L.L.' was in the possession of Barrymore, and that he had refused to tell me where it had come from.

nodes.description: The L.L. Letter to Sir Charles is the note from Mrs. Laura Lyons (initialled L.L.) that lured Sir Charles Baskerville to the Yew Alley gate on the night of his death, found in John Barrymore's possession in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: loaded revolvers

citations.quote: The courier and the young banker carried loaded revolvers

nodes.description: Loaded revolvers carried by the courier and the young banker for protection during the mountain journey hijacked by brigands in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Lodge Brand (Triangle in Circle)

citations.quote: mark on victim's arm
branded on McMurdo at initiation

nodes.description: A triangle-in-circle brand mark burned on the arm of initiates and found on a victim's body, linking the murder to the Scowrers lodge in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: looking-glasses / mirrors (sliding panels)

citations.quote: It was fitted and filled with looking-glasses at every angle of refraction

nodes.description: A room at Reed House fitted and filled with looking-glasses at every angle of refraction, creating a disorienting labyrinth of mirrors in The Sins of Prince Saradine (The Innocence of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Loose diamonds (without settings)

citations.quote: A very considerable hoard of precious stones, nearly all diamonds, and all of them loose, without any setting whatever.

nodes.description: A considerable hoard of loose diamonds without any settings, part of the eccentric collection of stripped precious objects at Glengyle Castle in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Heaps of loose snuff

citations.quote: Heaps and heaps of loose snuff, not kept in a horn, or even a pouch, but lying in heaps on the mantelpieces

nodes.description: Heaps of loose snuff lying on the mantelpieces without boxes or horns, part of the stripped collection mania of Lord Glengyle at the castle in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Lucy's Wedding Ring

citations.quote: It is a woman's wedding-ring. There has been a woman here.

nodes.description: Lucy's Wedding Ring is the plain gold ring found at the Lauriston Gardens murder scene in A Study in Scarlet that tells Holmes a woman was present; Holmes uses a newspaper advertisement for it as a trap to draw out Jefferson Hope.

nodes.label: Holmes's Magnifying Glass

citations.quote: He whipped a tape measure and a large round magnifying glass from his pocket.

nodes.description: Holmes's large round magnifying glass, used to examine footprints and clues at crime scenes, first produced at the Lauriston Gardens murder in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Mazarin Stone (Crown Diamond)

citations.quote: the great yellow Mazarin stone — one of the most precious jewels in Europe

nodes.description: The Mazarin Stone, also called the Crown Diamond, a priceless yellow gem stolen by Count Negretto Sylvius and ultimately recovered by Holmes in The Adventure of the Mazarin Stone.

nodes.label: Microscopic wheels and steel springs

citations.quote: curious little heaps of minute pieces of metal, some like steel springs and some in the form of microscopic wheels. As if they had gutted some mechanical toy.

nodes.description: Curious little heaps of microscopic steel springs and tiny wheels, found in the eccentric inventor Smythe's rooms and suggesting his mechanical preoccupations in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: empty medicine bottle (Arthur Carstairs suicide)

citations.quote: An empty medicine bottle lay on the floor. Across the table the body of the man in the brown dressing-gown lay amid his burst and gaping brown-paper parcels

nodes.description: The empty medicine bottle found on the floor beside the body of Arthur Carstairs, indicating the apparent means of his suicide in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Ming Saucer

citations.quote: that Ming saucer — the one he values so highly

nodes.description: A Ming saucer highly valued by the Baron, used as the pretext for Watson's visit to the villain's house in The Adventure of the Illustrious Client (The Case-Book of Sherlock Holmes).

nodes.label: Missing Boots of Sir Henry

citations.quote: "One of my boots had been stolen. A new brown boot—it was in my room last night and it's gone this morning."
Then one of the old black boots was stolen. "The boot!" said Holmes eagerly. "It's the scent they needed for the hound."

nodes.description: Sir Henry Baskerville's missing boots — first a new brown one, then an old black one — stolen by Stapleton to give the trained hound a scent to follow in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Missing Wedding Ring

citations.quote: missing from John Douglas's finger
body substitution

nodes.description: John Douglas's missing wedding ring, absent from the fingerless body in the moat at Birlstone, a key anomaly that alerts Holmes to the body-substitution scheme in The Valley of Fear.

nodes.label: The Moabite Cipher Document

citations.quote: half-sheet of thin notepaper, Moabite/Phoenician script in Chinese ink, concealing hidden water-activated Roman capitals message revealing the Piccadilly robbery loot location

nodes.description: A half-sheet of thin notepaper bearing a Moabite/Phoenician script in Chinese ink that conceals a hidden water-activated message in Roman capitals, the clue in John Thorndyke's Cases.

nodes.label: Dr. Mortimer's Walking Stick

citations.quote: "tell me, Watson, what do you make of our visitor's stick? Since we have been so unfortunate as to miss him and have no notion of his errand, this accidental souvenir becomes of importance."

nodes.description: Dr. Mortimer's walking stick, left behind at 221B Baker Street, which Holmes analyses to deduce its owner's profession and career before he arrives, opening The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: John Bankes's motor-car

citations.quote: John Bankes came to a stop beside them, radiant at his own steering-wheel.

nodes.description: John Bankes's motor-car, in which he arrives radiantly at his own steering-wheel to visit the hamlet in The Man with Two Beards (The Secret of Father Brown).

nodes.label: cutlass (Muscari's)

citations.quote: Muscari (with much boyish gratification) buckled on a kind of cutlass under his black cloak

nodes.description: Muscari's cutlass, buckled on with boyish gratification under his black cloak before the Alpine journey in The Paradise of Thieves (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Captain Musgrave's car

citations.section: The Worst Crime in the World
citations.quote: He offered to take me up in his own car to Musgrave Moss

nodes.description: Captain Musgrave's car, offered to take a friend north to Musgrave Moss, the journey that draws characters into the story in The Worst Crime in the World (The Secret of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Musgrave Ritual (ancient catechism)

citations.quote: "Whose was it?" "His who is gone." "Who shall have it?" "He who will come." "What was the month?" "The sixth from the first." "Where was the sun?" "Over the oak."

nodes.description: The Musgrave Ritual, an ancient catechism recited by the Musgrave family for generations, deciphered by Holmes to locate the hiding place of the Crown of Charles I in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: Mystery Note found on Garcia

citations.quote: a mysterious note found in Garcia's pocket

nodes.description: A mysterious note found in Garcia's pocket at the time of his murder, bearing cryptic instructions that form a central clue in The Adventure of Wisteria Lodge.

nodes.label: Nail scissors

citations.quote: he stopped by the table and picked up a small pair of nail scissors. 'Ah,' he said, with a sort of relief, 'this is what he did it with.'

nodes.description: A small pair of nail scissors picked up by Father Brown at a crime scene, identified as the key implement in a method of murder in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Oberstein's Naval Signals Book

citations.section: The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans
citations.quote: Oberstein's collection of naval signals

nodes.description: Oberstein's collection of naval signals, spy-craft material recovered in connection with the Bruce-Partington plans theft in The Adventure of the Bruce-Partington Plans.

nodes.label: Naval Treaty Document

citations.quote: a secret treaty... between England and Italy... I was alone copying the treaty out

nodes.description: The Naval Treaty document, a secret agreement between England and Italy stolen from the Foreign Office, whose recovery Holmes investigates in The Memoirs of Sherlock Holmes.

nodes.label: noiseless explosive formula (Hirsch's invention)

citations.quote: he had lately even discovered a noiseless explosive, the secret of which the Government was carefully guarding

nodes.description: Dr. Hirsch's secret formula for a noiseless explosive, carefully guarded by the French Government and the object of the spy's mission in The Duel of Dr Hirsch (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Oberon's fairy spear (stage prop)

citations.quote: He had already handed his spear in a lordly style, like a sceptre, to the piteous Parkinson

nodes.description: Oberon's fairy spear, a theatrical prop handed lordly-style like a sceptre to the actor Parkinson, who later uses it to kill his wife in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Ordnance Map of Dartmoor

citations.quote: Holmes leaned over this grotesque figure with much interest. "Is that a large scale map?" "Yes, it is the large Ordnance map of the district."

nodes.description: A large-scale Ordnance Survey map of Dartmoor used by Holmes and Watson to plan their movements and orientate themselves to the moor's geography in The Hound of the Baskervilles.

nodes.label: Oriental dagger (crooked knife inlaid with stones)

nodes.description: A curved Oriental dagger inlaid with stones, found hidden in the long grass of the garden and identified by Father Brown as the murder weapon in The Innocence of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: oriental dagger (crooked knife inlaid with stones)

nodes.label: Oriental musical instrument (silver/steel, like crooked violin)

citations.quote: a queer-looking musical instrument made of silver or steel, and shaped like a deformed or crooked violin. It was played with something like a silver comb.

nodes.description: A deformed or crooked silver or steel musical instrument resembling a violin, played with a bow, found among a character's possessions in The Secret of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: oriental musical instrument (silver/steel, like crooked violin)

nodes.label: Prince Otto's military sash / scarf (used as gag/murder weapon)

citations.section: The Fairy Tale of Father Brown
citations.quote: The sash or scarf had been unbound and lay rather crumpled by his side... They killed him with the sash... a strong, soft gag suddenly twisted round his head like a tourniquet. It was fully forty seconds before he even realized that the two Hungarian servants had done it, and that they had done it with his own military scarf.

nodes.description: Prince Otto's military sash or scarf, unbound and found crumpled beside him, used as both a gag and the murder weapon by his traitorous sentries in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: prince otto's military sash / scarf (used as gag/murder weapon)

nodes.label: Pantomime scenery (Blue Palace gates, Enchanted Grove)

normalized_terms: pantomime scenery (blue palace gates, enchanted grove)

nodes.label: Incomplete will (blue foolscap)

nodes.description: Pauline Stacey's incomplete will written on blue foolscap, leaving her money to the Eye of Apollo cult, cut short when the pen ran dry and she was sent to the lift in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Peacock green robe (Master's)

citations.quote: He was robed in peacock green and wore on his bald head a high cap of uncommon outline.

nodes.description: A peacock green robe and high cap of uncommon outline worn by the Master of the Mountain fortune-teller at his purple tent in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: The Emperor's Pearl

citations.quote: the great pearl, the gift of the Emperor

nodes.description: The Emperor's Pearl, a great gift jewel from a foreign monarch that Raffles steals during a royal visit in The Gift of the Emperor (The Amateur Cracksman).

nodes.label: Morstan Pearls

attributes.description: large lustrous pearls sent annually to Mary Morstan
attributes.count: six received by the time of the story

nodes.description: The Morstan Pearls are the series of very large lustrous pearls sent annually to Mary Morstan beginning six years before the events of The Sign of the Four, their origin ultimately traced by Holmes to the divided Agra treasure.

nodes.label: Pendragon's map (of river, mistaken for Pacific chart)

nodes.description: A brown sixteenth-century map with Tritons and little ships on a curly sea, mistaken by a character for a Pacific chart but actually a map of the Pendragon river and grounds in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Pendragon Tower (wooden, burning)

nodes.description: The old wooden Pendragon Tower alight with fire, a recurring curse-sign that Father Brown douses with a garden hose in The Perishing of the Pendragons (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

citations.quote: if you look at that pepper-pot I put on the table, you'll see a small hole. That's where Cray's bullet struck, shaking up the pepper and making the criminal sneeze.

nodes.description: A compromising photograph of a woman accompanied by a letter, the object of Holmes's mission for the King of Bohemia in A Scandal in Bohemia (The Adventures of Sherlock Holmes).

nodes.description: A small box of pills found at Valentin's elbow in his study, the means by which the great detective took his own life in The Innocence of Father Brown.

nodes.description: A scrap of pink society newspaper cutting handed to Father Brown containing a relevant report about events at Pendragon Park in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Pins and chart (General's study)

nodes.description: Pins stuck in a chart while General Outram smokes a long thin black cigar in his study, a visual detail of military habit observed by Father Brown in The Secret of Father Brown.

citations.quote: Playing-cards lay littered across the table or fluttered about the floor

nodes.description: Playing cards littered across the table and fluttered about the floor of Mr. Glass's room, found by Father Brown and the American investigator in The Absence of Mr. Glass (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Poison Bullet (hypodermic needle projectile)

nodes.description: A hypodermic needle projectile — a miniature two-inch brass cylinder with a needle at one end — used to administer poison at a distance in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: little glass bottle (poison)

nodes.description: A little glass bottle of poison whose contents cause a heavy face to turn clay-coloured when sniffed, found during an investigation in The Wisdom of Father Brown.

nodes.label: The Two Pills

nodes.description: Two pills produced by Holmes from a small cardboard box — one deadly, one harmless — used to demonstrate Jefferson Hope's poisoning method to the police in A Study in Scarlet.

nodes.label: Poison Thorn

attributes.description: long, sharp, black, with gummy substance

long, sharp, and black, with a glazed look near the point

nodes.description: The Poison Thorn is Tonga's dart in The Sign of the Four, a long sharp black thorn with a glazed poisoned point fired from a blowpipe through the locked window to kill Bartholomew Sholto.

nodes.label: Poisoned Cigar (Trichinopoly cheroot)

nodes.description: A Trichinopoly cheroot containing a chalky alkaloid deposit introduced near the small end, identified by Thorndyke as a means of delivering a slow poison in The Red Thumb Mark.

nodes.label: decanter of port (at Boulnois's elbow)

citations.quote: A decanter of port and a wineglass were at his elbow

nodes.description: A decanter of port and a wine glass at John Boulnois's elbow while he reads a penny dreadful, establishing his alibi at home in The Strange Crime of John Boulnois (The Wisdom of Father Brown).

nodes.label: portrait of Paul Arnhold (Chamberlain, white hair, black eyebrows)

citations.quote: he ruminated long before the portrait of a white-haired man with black eyebrows and a pink, painted sort of smile that seemed to contradict the black warning in his eyes.

nodes.description: A portrait of Paul Arnhold as Chamberlain, showing white hair, black eyebrows, and a pink painted smile, contemplated by a character in The Wisdom of Father Brown.
normalized_terms: portrait of paul arnhold (chamberlain, white hair, black eyebrows)

nodes.label: Post obit agreement

citations.quote: This young Musgrave wants to raise a big sum from us on a post obit on his old father in Northumberland.

nodes.description: A post obit agreement by which young Musgrave seeks to raise a large sum against his elderly father's estate in Northumberland, the financial transaction that draws attention to the family in The Secret of Father Brown.

nodes.label: Postman's uniform (Welkin's disguise)

citations.quote: 'He is dressed rather handsomely in red, blue and gold,' replied the priest promptly with precision

nodes.description: The red, blue, and gold postman's uniform worn by James Welkin as a disguise, making him invisible to witnesses as a habitual presence in The Invisible Man (The Innocence of Father Brown).

nodes.label: Potato sacks

nodes.description: Potato sacks used by the barber Wicks to conceal and transport Vaudrey's body from his shop to the river in The Secret of Father Brown.

citations.section: His Last Bow: The War Service of Sherlock Holmes
citations.quote: the Practical Handbook of Bee Culture — Holmes's magnum opus

nodes.description: The Practical Handbook of Bee Culture, Holmes's magnum opus written during his retirement, used as cover for his infiltration of Von Bork's spy network in His Last Bow.
